title: Block-Coordinate Methods and Restarting for Solving Extensive-Form Games

reviews.summary: This paper combines the local prox update technique with the extrapolated cyclic algorithm and proposes the ECyclicPDA algorithm. While the local prox update technique is well understood, the authors reinterpret it as a coordinate method (CM). Then, the method is combined with a new extrapolated method. Theoretical analysis shows that the proposed algorithm can converge at a rate of O(1/T).
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper presents a new understanding of the local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF. The combination of local OMD updates and extrapolated updates is original and interesting.
2. The theoretical results seem sound.
3. The paper is well-written.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. It is hard to understand why we want to reinterpret the well-known local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF to CM. As we know, people already compute the strategy in a bottom-up fashion in previous work [1, 2, 3]. So, I think the main contribution is the combination of the local update method and the extrapolated cyclic method.
2. However, the ECyclicPDA algorithm seems less efficient than the traditional MP algorithm. For every infoset, an extra traversal of the subgame rooted at the infoset is needed to compute the “extrapolated” vector. This could be infeasible for large-scale games.
3. The experimental results show that the proposed ECyclicPDA performs better than MP. The results are not surprising when considering that ECyclicPDA can be very time-consuming.
4. The algorithm does not compare with related optimistic OMD [4] algorithms for EFGs.


[1] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Optimistic regret minimization for extensive-form games via dilated distance-generating functions." Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019).
[2] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Better regularization for sequential decision spaces: Fast convergence rates for Nash, correlated, and team equilibria." arXiv preprint arXiv:2105.12954 (2021).
[3] Liu, Weiming, et al. "Equivalence analysis between counterfactual regret minimization and online mirror descent." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2022.
[4] Lee, Chung-Wei, Christian Kroer, and Haipeng Luo. "Last-iterate convergence in extensive-form games." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 34 (2021): 14293-14305.
reviews.questions: none
reviews.limitations: The authors have stated the limitations properly. No potential negative societal impact.

Summary:
This paper combines the local prox update technique with the extrapolated cyclic algorithm and proposes the ECyclicPDA algorithm. While the local prox update technique is well understood, the authors reinterpret it as a coordinate method (CM). Then, the method is combined with a new extrapolated method. Theoretical analysis shows that the proposed algorithm can converge at a rate of O(1/T).

Strengths:
1. This paper presents a new understanding of the local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF. The combination of local OMD updates and extrapolated updates is original and interesting.
2. The theoretical results seem sound.
3. The paper is well-written.

Weaknesses:
1. It is hard to understand why we want to reinterpret the well-known local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF to CM. As we know, people already compute the strategy in a bottom-up fashion in previous work [1, 2, 3]. So, I think the main contribution is the combination of the local update method and the extrapolated cyclic method.
2. However, the ECyclicPDA algorithm seems less efficient than the traditional MP algorithm. For every infoset, an extra traversal of the subgame rooted at the infoset is needed to compute the “extrapolated” vector. This could be infeasible for large-scale games.
3. The experimental results show that the proposed ECyclicPDA performs better than MP. The results are not surprising when considering that ECyclicPDA can be very time-consuming.
4. The algorithm does not compare with related optimistic OMD [4] algorithms for EFGs.


[1] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Optimistic regret minimization for extensive-form games via dilated distance-generating functions." Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019).
[2] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Better regularization for sequential decision spaces: Fast convergence rates for Nash, correlated, and team equilibria." arXiv preprint arXiv:2105.12954 (2021).

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

The authors propose ECyclicPDA, which integrates the local prox update mechanism with an extrapolated cyclic algorithmic framework. The local prox update technique itself is established in the literature, but the authors reframe it through the lens of coordinate methods (CM). This reframed technique is then merged with a newly developed extrapolation strategy. The theoretical analysis demonstrates convergence with a rate of O(1/T).

## Strengths

1. The paper offers a fresh perspective on the local update mechanisms inherent in OMD with dilated DGF. The novelty lies in combining local OMD updates with extrapolation techniques, which represents an interesting and original contribution.

2. The theoretical analysis appears rigorous and mathematically sound.

3. The manuscript is clearly presented and well-organized throughout.

## Weaknesses

1. The motivation for reformulating the previously established local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF as a coordinate method remains unclear. Given that prior work [1, 2, 3] already employs bottom-up computation of strategies, the primary innovation appears to be the fusion of the local update framework with the extrapolated cyclic methodology rather than the reinterpretation itself.

2. The ECyclicPDA algorithm exhibits computational inefficiency relative to the conventional MP algorithm. The requirement to perform an additional traversal of the subgame tree at each infoset to generate the "extrapolated" vector introduces substantial computational overhead that may prove prohibitive when scaling to realistic game sizes.

3. Although the experimental evaluation demonstrates that ECyclicPDA outperforms MP, these improvements are not particularly striking given the significant computational expense associated with the algorithm. The performance gains must be interpreted in light of the method's substantially higher computational cost.

4. The paper lacks empirical comparison with related optimistic OMD variants [4] designed for extensive-form games, which would provide important context for evaluating the practical advantages of the proposed approach.

## Questions

None.

## Limitations

The authors have adequately addressed the scope and limitations of their work. No concerns regarding potential negative societal impact are identified.

## Recommendation

**6: Weak Accept** - The paper presents technically sound results with moderate-to-high potential impact. The work demonstrates no significant issues with respect to experimental design, resource requirements, reproducibility, or ethical considerations. While the practical efficiency concerns and incomplete comparisons with related methods temper the contribution, the core technical contribution and theoretical results merit publication.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, a new first order method for solving extensive form games.  The main idea is to implement something in the spirit of coordinate descent where improving directions can be found by considering a part of the current iterate in isolation.  Empirical results show it generally outperforms other first-order methods and approaches the performance of CFR+.  Additionally, a heuristic to restart the process is introduced which improves the performance of both ECyclicPDA and CFR+
reviews.strengths:
ECyclicPDA is a nice contribution that takes the growing collection of FOMs for EFGs in a new direction.  The analysis seems to involve non-trivial technical innovations.  The empirical results are convining and show continued progress toward cloasing the gap with CFR-based approaches.

The restarting heuristic is also very interesting, and the ability to get performance improvements with CFR+ is particularly nice (and intuitive in hindsight).
reviews.weaknesses: As far as I can tell, the details of how restarting is implemented are never clearly explained.  The clearest explanation I can find is in the introduction on lines 88-91, but even taking that as the full specification, I don’t see where the tuning of the parameter about when to restart is specified.  It would also be nice to have a bit of explanation / intuition for how restarting is benefiting various algorithms.  For CFR+ I can see how resetting the regret sums and the averaging process could be beneficial.  For FOMs I’m less clear why it is useful.  Is it purely from resetting the averaging process?
reviews.questions: Please comment on the questions about resetting.
reviews.limitations: Adequate

Summary:
This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, a new first order method for solving extensive form games.  The main idea is to implement something in the spirit of coordinate descent where improving directions can be found by considering a part of the current iterate in isolation.  Empirical results show it generally outperforms other first-order methods and approaches the performance of CFR+.  Additionally, a heuristic to restart the process is introduced which improves the performance of both ECyclicPDA and CFR+

Strengths:
ECyclicPDA is a nice contribution that takes the growing collection of FOMs for EFGs in a new direction.  The analysis seems to involve non-trivial technical innovations.  The empirical results are convining and show continued progress toward cloasing the gap with CFR-based approaches.

The restarting heuristic is also very interesting, and the ability to get performance improvements with CFR+ is particularly nice (and intuitive in hindsight).

Weaknesses:
As far as I can tell, the details of how restarting is implemented are never clearly explained.  The clearest explanation I can find is in the introduction on lines 88-91, but even taking that as the full specification, I don’t see where the tuning of the parameter about when to restart is specified.  It would also be nice to have a bit of explanation / intuition for how restarting is benefiting various algorithms.  For CFR+ I can see how resetting the regret sums and the averaging process could be beneficial.  For FOMs I’m less clear why it is useful.  Is it purely from resetting the averaging process?

Questions:
Please comment on the questions about resetting.

Limitations:
Adequate

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

The paper presents ECyclicPDA, a novel first-order optimization method designed for solving extensive form games. The core approach draws inspiration from coordinate descent methodology, enabling the identification of descent directions by isolating and analyzing portions of the current solution. The experimental evaluation demonstrates that this method consistently outperforms competing first-order approaches and narrows the performance gap relative to CFR+ algorithms. Furthermore, the authors propose a restart mechanism that yields performance gains for both ECyclicPDA itself and for CFR+.

## Strengths

ECyclicPDA represents a valuable addition to the growing toolkit of first-order methods for extensive form games, offering a fresh perspective on the problem. The theoretical development incorporates several non-trivial technical contributions that advance the field. The empirical validation is compelling, demonstrating measurable progress in closing the performance gap between first-order methods and the established CFR+ baseline.

The restart heuristic itself merits particular recognition as an interesting contribution. Notably, its ability to enhance CFR+ performance is noteworthy and provides intuitive appeal upon reflection.

## Weaknesses

The presentation of the restart mechanism lacks sufficient clarity and completeness. While lines 88-91 in the introduction provide the most detailed exposition available, even this section does not fully specify the practical implementation details. Specifically, the paper does not adequately describe how the critical parameter governing restart timing is selected or tuned. Additionally, the paper would benefit from clearer intuition regarding the mechanisms through which restarting improves algorithm performance. For CFR+, the benefits are reasonably apparent—resetting accumulated regret sums and reinitializing the averaging procedure can clearly be advantageous. However, the rationale for why restarting enhances first-order methods is less transparent. Is the improvement attributable solely to resetting the averaging mechanism, or are other factors at play?

## Questions for Authors

Please provide clarification on the implementation and parameterization of the restart procedure, and offer additional insight into the underlying mechanisms explaining why restarting benefits the various algorithms studied.

## Limitations

The limitations discussion is adequate for the scope of the work.

## Overall Assessment and Recommendation

**Rating: 7 - Accept**

This paper presents technically sound research with substantial impact potential within its area. The work demonstrates good-to-excellent experimental rigor, and the authors have appropriately addressed reproducibility and ethical considerations. The combination of the novel ECyclicPDA algorithm and the broadly applicable restart heuristic represents a meaningful contribution to the field of game-theoretic optimization, warranting acceptance for publication.

reviews.summary: The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.
reviews.strengths: The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.
reviews.weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.
reviews.questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.
reviews.limitations: Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.

Strengths:
The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.

Weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.

Questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.

Limitations:
Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**

This paper addresses the problem of solving extended-form games (EFGs) through the application of extrapolated block-coordinate descent techniques, establishing an O(1/T) convergence guarantee. The authors additionally demonstrate that incorporating a restarting mechanism can yield performance comparable to established state-of-the-art methods such as CFR+. The core technical contribution appears to be an adaptation of the CODER algorithm by Song [37], extended from standard coordinate descent to block-coordinate descent and tailored to the specific structure of linear functional spaces and treeplexi. In essence, the methodology mirrors CODER in its fundamental structure but generalizes from scalar coordinate updates to blocks of "separable" variables. For the EFG application, the work specializes to bilinear score functions and 2-player treeplexi, substituting L1-norm proximal operations for CODER's L2-norm approach. The implementation leverages the recursive geometry of treeplexi to achieve efficient linear-time computation.

**Strengths:**

A key merit of this work is demonstrating how to instantiate the CODER algorithm efficiently on EFGs with block-wise L1 proximal operators, and showing that restarting can achieve empirical performance on par with CFR+. The paper also furnishes an O(1/T) convergence rate that exhibits independence from the total number of variables, which is a useful property. Overall, the manuscript illustrates that the straightforward application of CODER with extrapolation and L1 specialization yields competitive results in the EFG setting.

**Weaknesses:**

A significant concern is whether the block-coordinate extension genuinely represents a novel contribution. Upon closer inspection of the CODER proof, the analysis does not inherently depend on coordinate-wise updates; rather, it naturally accommodates block-wise updates. CODER's original presentation employs d-dimensional notation for convenience but already claims to perform block coordinate descent in its abstract. For the EFG case where d=2, the independence from the number of variables follows naturally as a consequence of having a constant number of blocks (assuming the proximal operation is well-defined). Consequently, the theoretical guarantee regarding variable-count independence is not particularly surprising. Additionally, the work does not relax the separability assumption, since the 2-player treeplex structure is inherently separable into two blocks. The primary distinction lies in the analysis of the L1 proximal operator and the corresponding theoretical treatment.

Moreover, while the empirical success with restarting is encouraging, this component lacks theoretical justification. Without the restarting mechanism, the method—which has theoretical guarantees—does not outperform existing state-of-the-art approaches. With restarting, the full algorithm achieves competitiveness but does not substantially exceed CFR+ in performance. These considerations suggest the paper occupies a borderline position. That said, the theoretical analysis appears rigorous and sound, warranting a borderline acceptance.

**Questions and Concerns:**

1. (Figure 2) The experimental design appears asymmetric: your method receives step-size optimization while baseline methods do not. To ensure a fair comparison, either all methods should use fixed step-sizes or all should have their step-sizes tuned. Without this adjustment, it remains unclear whether the proposed method's advantage over MP is genuine or an artifact of the tuning disparity.

2. (Figure 4) The abrupt vertical discontinuities in the plotted curves suggest either a computational artifact or an incorrect specification of the optimal value. This requires clarification.

3. (Algorithm 1) The presentation could benefit from explicit parentheses and notation indicating which quantities are maintained throughout the computation, enhancing readability and precision.

**Limitations:**

The paper does not include a dedicated limitations section.

**Overall Assessment:**

The work presents technically sound analysis with a moderate contribution level. The core ideas are well-executed, though the novelty relative to existing block-coordinate descent frameworks is limited. The empirical results are promising when restarting is employed, though this lacks theoretical support. Evaluation and reproducibility concerns are minimal.

**Rating:**

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.

Strengths:
The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.

Weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. 1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. 2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.

reviews.summary:
This paper develops a cyclic block-coordinate-descent-like method for two-player zero-sum extensive-form games (EFG). Such methods for EFG are  difficult due to non-separable nature of block structure of the problem. The decision problem for a player in a EFG can be formulated using Treeplex, for which regularizing functions can be constructed through the framework of dilated regularizers. These dilated regularizing functions allow recursive prox computations. This paper utilizes this frame work to develop an extrapolated cyclic algorithm to perform pseudo-block updates.  They demonstrate O(1/T) convergence rate for two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium  using this method, and provide a specific algorithmic implementation which shows that runtime of the proposed method  is independent of the number of blocks. 

Experimental evaluation on EFG benchmark games is performed using three different dilated regularizers -dilated entropy, dilated global entropy, and dilated $\ell_2$ with different block construction strategies. Experiments demonstrate the benefit of using blocks over non-block based approach in  some games, and no significant diffference between different block construction methods in others.  The results show improved  performance over the state of the art first order method (Mirror-Prox). Further, they introduce a restarting heuristic which speeds up the proposed method as well as the baselines.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This work presents a cyclic block-coordinate-descent variant designed to solve two-player zero-sum extensive-form games (EFG). The primary challenge addressed is the non-separability inherent in the block structure of such problems. The authors formulate player decision problems using the Treeplex framework and leverage dilated regularizers to enable recursive proximal computations. An extrapolated cyclic algorithm is then constructed to execute pseudo-block updates. The method achieves O(1/T) convergence guarantees for computing two-player zero-sum Nash equilibria, with a key property being that computational runtime scales independently of the number of blocks. The experimental validation employs three distinct dilated regularizer variants—dilated entropy, dilated global entropy, and dilated ℓ2—across multiple block construction strategies. Results indicate that block-based approaches yield improvements over non-block methods in certain game instances, while different block partitioning strategies show comparable performance in others. The proposed approach demonstrates superior empirical performance relative to the current state-of-the-art first-order method (Mirror-Prox), and the authors further propose a restarting strategy that enhances convergence speed for both their method and competing approaches.

## Strengths

The manuscript is well-organized and clearly written, with sufficient background material provided to make the content accessible to readers without deep prior expertise in the area.

The algorithmic contribution appears to represent a genuine advance, though I acknowledge limited personal familiarity with this particular research domain.

The experimental validation is persuasive, with the proposed method demonstrating clear improvements over state-of-the-art first-order methods across the tested benchmarks.

## Weaknesses

A significant gap exists at line 105, where the authors assert that "randomized coordinate methods are not applicable to our setting" without having previously justified this claim anywhere in the paper. This assertion requires explicit explanation and justification.

The related work section would benefit from inclusion of discussion regarding [i], which presents primal-dual coordinate methods for bilinear saddle-point problems and appears relevant to the current work.

[i] Carmon et al. Coordinate Methods for Matrix Games

## Limitations

The authors acknowledge an open theoretical question regarding why the restarting heuristic proves effective with regularizers beyond ℓ2. This limitation is adequately discussed and appropriately framed.

## Recommendation

This paper represents technically sound work with substantial impact potential within its subarea. The combination of strong theoretical contributions, convincing experimental results, and solid presentation quality, coupled with adequate treatment of limitations and reproducibility considerations, supports acceptance. The work merits publication and will likely generate interest within the relevant research community.

**Rating: 7 - Accept**: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one area, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This work proposes a cyclic coordinate descent method to solve the two-player zero-sum extended form game (EFG) and derives the convergence.
reviews.strengths: To me, solving problems with non-separable constraints by coordinate-descent-type methods is novel and interesting. Therefore, I believe that this is indeed a contribution.
reviews.weaknesses:
I'm not familiar with this area so please forgave me if I made some mistakes.

1. Possibly expensive computational cost per iteration. Following the literature survey in the manuscript, I read reference [1] which studies coordinate descent for solving optimization problems with non-separable non-smooth objective functions. I found that in both the algorithm in [1] and the algorithm in this paper, although only partial gradient is needed at each update, the proximal step with respect to the whole nonsmooth function is needed. More specifically, in Lines 8 and 11, the argmin step might be difficult and computationally expensive since the argmin step is over the whole X and Y and involve all the blocks.

2.  This concern follows the first one but focuses on the experiments: the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms may be unfair. The current comparison is in terms of the number of full gradient computations. However, gradient computation is not the only computation cost, Lines 8 and 11 also cause computation costs. I would suggest a comparison in terms of wall clock time.

reviews.limitations: I do not see any potential negative societal impact.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Review

## Summary
The paper presents a cyclic coordinate descent approach for addressing two-player zero-sum games in extended form, along with corresponding convergence guarantees.

## Strengths
I find the application of coordinate descent methodology to handle problems featuring non-separable constraints to be both novel and compelling. This represents a meaningful advancement in the field, and I believe the work makes a solid contribution to the literature.

## Weaknesses

1. **Potential computational burden per iteration**: Upon reviewing the referenced literature (particularly reference [1], which examines coordinate descent for non-smooth optimization with non-separable objectives), I observed a notable similarity in algorithmic structure. Specifically, while both the cited work and the proposed algorithm require only partial gradient computations at each step, they both necessitate solving a proximal operator with respect to the complete non-smooth function. This is evident in Lines 8 and 11, where the optimization steps involve the full decision spaces X and Y across all variable blocks, potentially rendering these subproblems difficult to solve and computationally intensive.

2. **Experimental comparison concerns**: Building on the computational cost issue above, the experimental evaluation warrants closer examination. The current benchmarking methodology—measuring performance via the number of full gradient evaluations—may not provide a complete picture of computational efficiency. Since Lines 8 and 11 introduce additional computational overhead beyond gradient calculations, I recommend supplementing the current comparison with wall-clock time measurements to provide a more comprehensive assessment of practical performance.

## Questions
None.

## Limitations
I do not identify any significant potential for negative societal impact.

## Rating
**5: Borderline accept**: This is a technically sound contribution where the merits supporting acceptance marginally outweigh the limitations (such as incomplete experimental evaluation). The work should be accepted, though the noted concerns should be addressed in future revisions.

Summary:
This work proposes a cyclic coordinate descent method to solve the two-player zero-sum extended form game (EFG) and derives the convergence.

Strengths:
To me, solving problems with non-separable constraints by coordinate-descent-type methods is novel and interesting. Therefore, I believe that this is indeed a contribution.

Weaknesses:
I'm not familiar with this area so please forgave me if I made some mistakes.

1. Possibly expensive computational cost per iteration. Following the literature survey in the manuscript, I read reference [1] which studies coordinate descent for solving optimization problems with non-separable non-smooth objective functions. I found that in both the algorithm in [1] and the algorithm in this paper, although only partial gradient is needed at each update, the proximal step with respect to the whole nonsmooth function is needed. More specifically, in Lines 8 and 11, the argmin step might be difficult and computationally expensive since the argmin step is over the whole X and Y and involve all the blocks.

2.  This concern follows the first one but focuses on the experiments: the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms may be unfair. The current comparison is in terms of the number of full gradient computations. However, gradient computation is not the only computation cost, Lines 8 and 11 also cause computation costs. I would suggest a comparison in terms of wall clock time.

no

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFG) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. To enable pseudo block-wise updates, it takes use of the recursive nature of the proximal update caused by dilated regularizers. A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.

Strengths:
In summary, the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art techniques in some scenarios. The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of their proposed ECyclicPDA approach.

Weaknesses:
The execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed in the paper. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison in order to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Although it is mentioned that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks, a comprehensive evaluation is still lacking. Additionally, the paper overlooks competitive alternatives like DCFR, despite making references to these methods.

Questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?

Limitations:
During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes the Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA), a novel algorithmic approach for solving large-scale extensive-form games. The method leverages the recursive structure inherent in proximal operators with dilated regularizers to facilitate pseudo block-wise coordinate updates. Additionally, the authors introduce a restarting mechanism that shows promise for accelerating both their cyclic algorithm and existing methods such as mirror prox and variants of CFR+.

## Strengths

The ECyclicPDA algorithm represents a meaningful contribution to the literature on sequential game solving, offering a fresh perspective on coordinate descent and first-order optimization techniques for EFGs. The proposed method demonstrates competitive or superior empirical performance relative to several existing state-of-the-art approaches in certain test cases. The paper provides a thorough review of related work on coordinate descent and first-order methods, effectively positioning the novelty and potential advantages of the ECyclicPDA framework within the broader context of the field.

## Weaknesses

A significant limitation of the paper is the insufficient treatment of computational complexity and wall-clock runtime comparisons between ECyclicPDA and competing modern algorithms. Such an analysis is critical for assessing the practical viability and real-world applicability of the proposed method. While the authors note that ECyclicPDA's runtime exhibits independence from the number of blocks, this observation lacks the empirical validation and detailed investigation necessary to substantiate such claims. Furthermore, the paper fails to adequately address or compare against relevant alternatives in the literature, notably DCFR, which is mentioned but not thoroughly examined.

## Questions and Concerns

1) The authors state that "for the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, prior work by Liu et al. in "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" appears to present scenarios where first-order OMD-based methods achieve superior performance to CFR+. This claim may warrant clarification or qualification.

2) What is the practical strength of the assumption regarding strongly convex functions being "nice," as discussed on page 5 in the section on dilated regularizers? How restrictive is this requirement in practice?

3) The appendix contains three figures with identical captions. It remains unclear whether these visualizations correspond to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging schemes, respectively, and this ambiguity should be resolved.

## Limitations

The experimental evaluation reveals that ECyclicPDA achieves superior performance relative to CFR+ exclusively in the Battleship game—representing the sole instance of competitive results with PCFR+, which nonetheless remains the best-performing algorithm overall. The paper does not provide sufficient intuition or explanation for why ECyclicPDA specifically outperforms CFR+ on this particular benchmark, making it difficult to understand the scope and generalizability of the improvements.

## Recommendation

**5: Borderline accept** — This paper presents technically sound work with sufficient merit to warrant publication, though with notable limitations. The strengths of the algorithmic contribution and theoretical framework are offset by incomplete empirical evaluation and limited experimental scope. The work would benefit from more comprehensive computational analysis and broader experimental validation, but the core contributions are sufficiently solid to merit acceptance despite these reservations.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFG) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. To enable pseudo block-wise updates, it takes use of the recursive nature of the proximal update caused by dilated regularizers. A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.

Strengths:
In summary, the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art techniques in some scenarios. The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of their proposed ECyclicPDA approach.

Weaknesses:
The execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed in the paper. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison in order to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Although it is mentioned that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks, a comprehensive evaluation is still lacking. Additionally, the paper overlooks competitive alternatives like DCFR, despite making references to these methods.

Questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?

Limitations:
During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

The method features a favorable theoretical convergence rate but the empirical results are often comparable or worse than existing methods in terms of the duality gap.

- The introduction to the problem is very approachable even to domain non-experts.

- Only a theoretical convergence rate and not an actual computation cost measured in units of time is reported.
- The plots lack error bars (unless no variance is possible to obtain).
- The contributions are explained indirectly. They could be listed in a more compact and more explicit form (e.g., a numbered list) to allow for critical assessment.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes a novel block-coordinate-based approach for computing equilibria in Extensive-Form Games. The authors provide clear motivation and intuitive explanations for their methodology and validate it empirically across four game instances, measuring performance via the primal-dual gap metric. While the method demonstrates attractive theoretical convergence guarantees, the experimental results generally show performance that is either comparable to or falls short of existing baselines in terms of closing the duality gap.

## Strengths

- The manuscript is clearly written with no apparent grammatical or stylistic deficiencies.
- The problem formulation and background material are presented accessibly, making the work understandable to readers outside the specialized domain.
- The paper includes empirical investigation of design choices related to block construction strategy selection.
- The method exhibits strong theoretical convergence properties.

## Weaknesses

- The empirical evaluation reveals that the proposed method consistently underperforms relative to PCFR+ across the tested scenarios (as evident in Figures 2 and 4), yet the paper does not provide adequate explanation or justification for this gap.
- The evaluation focuses exclusively on theoretical convergence rates without reporting practical computational costs measured in wall-clock time or other standard computational units.
- Uncertainty quantification is absent from the presented plots; error bars or confidence intervals should be included if variance estimates are available.
- The key contributions of the work are presented in a dispersed manner throughout the text. A more explicit and structured presentation (such as a numbered enumeration) would facilitate clearer understanding and critical evaluation of the contributions.

## Questions

- What is the actual runtime comparison between the proposed method and competing approaches in practice?

## Rebuttal Response

Upon reviewing the authors' rebuttal, I find that their responses adequately address my raised concerns and provide reasonable explanations for the observed empirical limitations. The logical justification offered for the method's practical performance has increased my confidence in the work. However, I maintain a measured assessment given my limited depth of expertise in this particular research area.

## Limitations

- The treatment of the paper's limitations is relatively superficial, amounting largely to a brief acknowledgment that the effect of game type characteristics on algorithmic performance remains poorly understood.

## Recommendation

**5: Borderline accept** - This is a technically sound contribution where the merits of acceptance moderately outweigh the concerns, particularly given the constraints in experimental scope. The paper makes a legitimate theoretical contribution despite empirical limitations.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper introduces a novel method for solving Extensive-Form Games based on a block-coordinate approach. The authors motivate and explain their idea and experimentally evaluate its performance in terms of primal-dual gap in four different games. The method features a favorable theoretical convergence rate but the empirical results are often comparable or worse than existing methods in terms of the duality gap.

Strengths:
- The paper is well written and free of grammar and stylistic issues.
- The introduction to the problem is very approachable even to domain non-experts.
- Experimental validation of the block construction strategy choice.
- Favorable converge properties.

Weaknesses:
- It seems that in empirical studies the method is consistently worse than PCFR+ (Fig 2 and 4). I have a difficulty to find a complete justification for this in the paper.
- Only a theoretical convergence rate and not an actual computation cost measured in units of time is reported.
- The plots lack error bars (unless no variance is possible to obtain).
- The contributions are explained indirectly. They could be listed in a more compact and more explicit form (e.g., a numbered list) to allow for critical assessment.

Questions:
- How does the run-time of the tested methods compare in practice?

*** Rebuttal Acknowledgment ***

I have read the author's rebuttal. The answer covers my questions well and the explanation the authors provides logical justification of the potential shortcomings. Therefore, I am of the opinion that the paper is sound and I increased my rating accordingly. I do not go higher due to my very limited familiarity with the subfield.

Limitations:
- The discussion of limitation is not very thorough and it is mostly limited to a mention of a lack of understanding of the game type effect on the performance.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

Summary:
The paper introduces a neural implicit representation based collaborative SLAM technique. They introduce a learnable feature encoding for each point in the 3d scene based on neural rendering subsequently using it for depth and color map estimation. They introduce a centralized learning strategy to share MLPs and learn better weights. They also introduce a LM algorithm based global pose optimization and key frame centric point cloud adjustment.

Strengths:
The federated learning strategy improves the neural rendering performance. Map refinement based on keyframe centric neural point field.
By encoding scene geometry and appearance with 3D points, when camera poses are optimized, these features are easily adjusted as well.

Weaknesses:
1. The paper only evaluates on one synthetic datasets. It would be nice to see evaluations on more datasets 
2. Better and more robust neural rendering techniques could have been used to improve the radiance, depth and color rendering

Questions:
1. The paper is not clear whether it includes neural field generation time in tracking/mapping time while comparing with Nice SLAM. 
2. The paper lacks citations. For example, in the mapping and tracking section, a similar technique is used in Nice SLAM and I see no mention of it.

Limitations:
1.The paper does produce SOTA results on some of the scenes of the dataset chosen. No explanation is given to why it doesn't produce better results on the rest. 
2. The paper is not novel enough. It rather combines the best of many other works and produces good results.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This work presents a collaborative SLAM framework grounded in neural implicit representations. The authors propose learnable feature encodings for individual 3D scene points, which are subsequently leveraged for neural rendering to produce depth and color predictions. A centralized learning framework is introduced to facilitate MLP weight sharing and improve overall rendering quality. Additionally, the paper incorporates a Levenberg-Marquardt optimization procedure for refining camera poses globally and implements keyframe-based point cloud refinement strategies.

Strengths:
The federated learning approach demonstrates meaningful improvements in neural rendering outcomes. The map refinement procedure built around keyframe-centric neural point fields represents a solid contribution. The encoding of both scene geometry and appearance directly into 3D point representations enables straightforward feature adjustment during camera pose optimization.

Weaknesses:
1. The experimental validation is limited to a single synthetic benchmark. Evaluation across multiple diverse datasets would substantially strengthen the empirical claims and demonstrate broader applicability.
2. The paper could benefit from incorporating more sophisticated and stable neural rendering methodologies to enhance the quality of radiance, depth, and color reconstruction.

Questions:
1. Clarity is needed regarding whether neural field generation computational overhead is factored into the reported tracking and mapping runtime comparisons with Nice SLAM.
2. The citation coverage appears incomplete. Notably, similar methodological approaches are employed in Nice SLAM for mapping and tracking operations, yet this prior work receives insufficient attribution.

Limitations:
1. While the method achieves state-of-the-art performance on certain scenes within the evaluated dataset, the paper provides no analysis of why performance degrades on remaining scenes or what factors contribute to this inconsistency.
2. The conceptual novelty is somewhat constrained. The work primarily synthesizes established techniques from multiple sources into a cohesive system, albeit one that produces competitive results.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically sound with moderate-to-high potential impact, containing no substantial issues regarding experimental rigor, computational resources, reproducibility, or ethical dimensions.

reviews.summary: The paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM system developed by the authors. CP-SLAM incorporates neural point-based odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. Additionally, the authors propose a novel neural point 3D scene representation with keyframes to enhance map fusion and adjustment. They introduce innovative learning and optimization frameworks to ensure accurate and consistent 3D mapping for cooperative localization and mapping. The performance of CP-SLAM is evaluated on various indoor RGB-D sequences, showcasing its superior mapping and camera tracking capabilities. The contributions made by the authors significantly advance the field of SLAM and offer state-of-the-art solutions.
reviews.strengths:
1. CP-SLAM introduces a novel neural point-based representation, enabling dense collaborative neural SLAM.
2. CP-SLAM retains the front-end and back-end modules like traditional SLAM systems, providing a comprehensive pipeline.
3. CP-SLAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both localization and reconstruction. The CP-SLAM system supports both single-agent and multi-agent modes, allowing for versatile evaluation.
4. The system is evaluated using datasets based on the Replica scenes, providing a realistic and representative testing environment.
CP-SLAM is compared against recent neural implicit RGB-D SLAM methods in single-agent experiments, showcasing its performance in loop closure.
5. In the two-agent experiments, CP-SLAM is compared with a traditional method, highlighting its collaborative capabilities.
6. The ablation studies are conducted to demonstrate the importance of different modules in the proposed system.
7. The implementation details, such as GPU specifications and parameter settings, are provided, ensuring reproducibility.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. CP-SLAM requires significant GPU resources to handle multiple image sequences. The relative pose computation in loop closure relies on existing rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes and lead to map fusion drifting.
2. The comparison with traditional methods in the two-agent experiments may not fully represent the performance of other collaborative neural SLAM approaches.
3. CP-SLAM's performance is limited by its GPU resource demand, making it less suitable for resource-constrained devices or environments.
4. Currently, a large-scale experiment is not conducted, but for multi-agents, this is an important point to consider.
reviews.questions:
1. How does CP-SLAM handle scalability in terms of the size and complexity of the datasets?
2. Can CP-SLAM be applied to real-world datasets to evaluate its performance in practical scenarios?
3. Are there any specific limitations or challenges in the implementation of CP-SLAM that need to be addressed?
4. Can Nicer-SLAM and DROID-SLAM be compared as a contrast?
5. The article mentioned "Naturally, it is expected that the neural point cloud layout should be rearranged following global pose graph optimization. However, a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment. To tackle this limitation, we propose a keyframe-centric neural point field, where each 3D point is associated with a keyframe". Can the authors better explain why a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment? 

Sincerely,

Strengths:

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: CP-SLAM

## Summary

This work introduces CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM framework that integrates neural point-based odometry with loop closure detection, sub-map alignment, and pose graph optimization. A key contribution is the proposal of a keyframe-centric neural point representation designed to facilitate effective map fusion and global refinement. The authors develop specialized learning and optimization procedures to achieve robust and coherent 3D reconstruction across multi-agent scenarios. Experimental validation on indoor RGB-D benchmarks demonstrates competitive performance in both camera localization and scene reconstruction tasks, representing meaningful progress in neural SLAM research.

## Strengths

1. The introduction of a keyframe-centric neural point representation is a valuable innovation that enables practical collaborative neural SLAM with dense reconstruction capabilities.

2. The system architecture maintains the classical SLAM pipeline structure with distinct front-end and back-end components, providing a well-organized and interpretable framework.

3. Experimental results demonstrate improvements over existing neural implicit RGB-D SLAM baselines in both localization accuracy and reconstruction quality. The framework's flexibility to operate in both single-agent and multi-agent configurations enables comprehensive empirical assessment.

4. The use of Replica-based datasets provides controlled, realistic evaluation scenarios with well-characterized ground truth.

5. Single-agent comparisons against recent neural implicit methods effectively demonstrate loop closure performance capabilities.

6. Multi-agent experiments provide evidence of collaborative functionality, though with limited baseline comparisons.

7. Ablation studies systematically validate the contribution of individual system components.

8. Sufficient implementation details regarding computational specifications and hyperparameter configurations are documented to support reproducibility efforts.

## Weaknesses

1. The system exhibits substantial GPU memory and computational requirements for processing multiple image sequences, which constrains practical applicability. Furthermore, the loop closure pose estimation relies on rendering-based optimization techniques that may produce inaccurate results when viewpoint differences are substantial, potentially introducing drift during map fusion operations.

2. The multi-agent evaluation relies primarily on comparison with traditional SLAM methods rather than alternative collaborative neural SLAM approaches, limiting the scope of competitive analysis.

3. The computational intensity of CP-SLAM presents a significant practical limitation, restricting deployment to well-resourced settings and reducing suitability for edge computing or resource-limited platforms.

4. The paper does not present large-scale experimental validation, which represents a notable gap given the importance of scalability assessment for multi-agent systems.

## Questions and Clarifications Sought

1. How does the system address scalability challenges as dataset size and structural complexity increase?

2. Would performance validation on authentic real-world datasets be feasible, and how might results differ from the current Replica-based evaluation?

3. Are there particular implementation challenges or fundamental constraints that warrant explicit discussion?

4. Could the authors provide comparative analysis with Nicer-SLAM and DROID-SLAM as additional baselines?

5. The paper states that "world-centric point cloud maps obviously cannot allow adjustment following global pose graph optimization" and proposes keyframe-centric representations as a solution. A more thorough explanation of why world-centric representations inherently preclude such adjustment would strengthen the motivation for the proposed approach.

## Limitations

The authors address limitations within the concluding discussion.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents technically sound work with moderate-to-high impact potential. The proposed neural point representation and collaborative SLAM framework represent meaningful contributions to the field. The experimental evaluation is reasonably comprehensive with adequate implementation transparency. While computational requirements and limited multi-agent baseline comparisons present notable concerns, these do not constitute fundamental flaws that would warrant rejection. The work advances the state of neural SLAM methodology and provides sufficient novelty and rigor for publication.

## Recommendation

**6: Weak Accept** – The paper is technically competent with meaningful contributions and appropriate experimental validation. While there are areas for improvement regarding scalability evaluation and computational efficiency, no major concerns exist regarding experimental rigor, resource availability, reproducibility, or ethical considerations that would prevent acceptance.

They introduce innovative learning and optimization frameworks to ensure accurate and consistent 3D mapping for cooperative localization and mapping. The performance of CP-SLAM is evaluated on various indoor RGB-D sequences, showcasing its superior mapping and camera tracking capabilities. The contributions made by the authors significantly advance the field of SLAM and offer state-of-the-art solutions.

Strengths:
1. CP-SLAM introduces a novel neural point-based representation, enabling dense collaborative neural SLAM.

They use a loop closure detection based on learned features (NetVLAD) also for sub-map alignment. They propose a central federated learning based averaging and refinement of the distributed MLPs after submap alignment.
reviews.strengths: The use of point based features nicely connects the generalization and compression of implicit mapping with the ability to do efficient map refinement. The results on tracking and reconstruction show a large improvement compared to existing works.
reviews.weaknesses:
The explanations are sometimes a bit vague. E.g. line 101 what does it mean to incrementally project a pixel to a 3d location?
The initialization of system is not described well.
While the authors describe the averaging and finetuning of the MLP weights, but do not discuss if and how the point features are shared between agents.

Why not evaluate on real data?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors propose a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline, where the map is represented by an implicit field based on Point-NERF that are transformed together with their keyframes allowing pose graph optimization. They use a loop closure detection based on learned features (NetVLAD) also for sub-map alignment. They propose a central federated learning based averaging and refinement of the distributed MLPs after submap alignment.

Strengths:
The use of point based features nicely connects the generalization and compression of implicit mapping with the ability to do efficient map refinement. The results on tracking and reconstruction show a large improvement compared to existing works.

Weaknesses:
The explanations are sometimes a bit vague. E.g. line 101 what does it mean to incrementally project a pixel to a 3d location?
The initialization of system is not described well.
While the authors describe the averaging and finetuning of the MLP weights, but do not discuss if and how the point features are shared between agents.
Evaluation was only done on synthetic data.

Questions:
Why is there another local finetuning and weight averaging done after the central averaging and finetuning?
How is the finetuning done after pose graph optimization? Is it the same as for mapping e.g. eq 9?
Would it not be possible to run ORB-SLAM3 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.11898.pdf) in a multi-map setting using depth from RGB-D as a baseline?
Why not evaluate on real data?
How is the TSDF fusion done to generate the final mesh?
What is the average baseline between frames in the dataset?
How long are the trajectories?

After rebuttal: The authors addressed all of my questions. Thank you.

Limitations:
One of the main limitations of framerate/view point change is addressed by the authors.. an initial experiment to showcase the effect would have been nice though.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This paper introduces a collaborative RGB-D SLAM system that leverages implicit neural field representations, specifically building upon Point-NERF, where these fields are jointly optimized with their associated keyframe poses through pose graph optimization. The authors employ learned feature-based loop closure detection via NetVLAD to facilitate sub-map alignment and propose a federated learning approach for distributed MLP weight averaging and refinement across agents following sub-map registration.

## Strengths

The integration of point-based feature representations effectively bridges the gap between the generalization capabilities and compression benefits of implicit mapping with the practical efficiency of map refinement operations. The experimental results demonstrate substantial performance gains in both localization accuracy and reconstruction quality relative to competing state-of-the-art methods.

## Weaknesses

Several presentation issues diminish clarity throughout the manuscript. Specifically, certain technical descriptions lack sufficient detail—for instance, the statement at line 101 regarding incremental pixel-to-3D projection requires clarification regarding the actual computational procedure. The system initialization procedure is inadequately described, leaving readers uncertain about practical implementation details. Additionally, while the paper addresses MLP weight averaging and subsequent refinement procedures, it fails to clarify whether and how point-level features are exchanged or synchronized among collaborative agents. The experimental validation is limited to synthetic datasets, which raises concerns about real-world applicability.

## Questions Requiring Clarification

1. What is the motivation for performing an additional round of local refinement and weight averaging subsequent to the central averaging and refinement step?
2. Following pose graph optimization, how is the refinement procedure executed? Does it employ the same optimization formulation as the mapping stage (e.g., Equation 9)?
3. Could ORB-SLAM3 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.11898.pdf) serve as a viable baseline when adapted for multi-map scenarios using RGB-D depth information?
4. What prevents evaluation on real-world datasets?
5. Could the authors provide technical details regarding the TSDF fusion methodology used for final mesh generation?
6. What constitutes the typical baseline distance between consecutive frames in the experimental dataset?
7. What is the typical trajectory length in the evaluation sequences?

**Post-rebuttal note:** The authors have satisfactorily addressed all questions raised during review. The clarifications provided are appreciated.

## Limitations

The paper identifies and discusses one primary limitation concerning frame rate and viewpoint variation sensitivity; however, the inclusion of preliminary experimental results demonstrating this limitation's practical impact would have strengthened the contribution.

## Overall Assessment

Rating: **6 - Weak Accept**

This work represents a technically sound contribution with moderate-to-high potential impact. The evaluation methodology, resource requirements, reproducibility considerations, and ethical aspects raise no significant concerns.

reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.
reviews.strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.

This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.

Strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.

The motivation is clear and the method is very well engineered, but from novelty perspective, all the components in CP-SLAM are already there with some incremental modification: e.g. the scene representation is from PointNeRF, Loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model, etc.

Questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. 4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.

Yes
