reviews.summary: The authors propose an inducing-point variational Bayes approximation for linear inverse problems. They study the frequentist properties of the arising variational posterior approximation. Specifically, they prove contraction rates for the variational posterior, both in the mildly and severely ill posed case. Moreover, they illustrate the practical relevance of their results with a number of synthetic data experiments.
reviews.strengths: The paper is well-written and its objectives and scope are clear. It provides an important contribution. As far as I am aware, frequentist properties of variational posteriors arising as inducing-point approximations in linear inverse problems have not been studied explicitly before. Such properties and contraction rates are useful in that they illustrate how the choice of the prior and the number of inducing points in the approximation impacts the statistical properties of the variational posterior. In that sense, they can provide useful guidance for the user.
reviews.weaknesses: I am not exactly sure if the comparison to the existing literature provided by the authors is substantial enough – I address this among my questions to the authors.
reviews.questions:
In 2020, three papers were published in the Annals of Statistics providing a comprehensive and general study of the frequentist properties of variational posteriors. Two of those papers are cited by the authors as references [2] and [56]. Another one I would add to this list is “$\alpha$-VARIATIONAL INFERENCE WITH STATISTICAL GUARANTEES” by Yang, Pati, Bhattacharya. I wonder if the authors would explain whether the results of those papers could be applied to the variational GP approximations for linear inverse problems, as considered in the authors’ submitted work. If they could, then I would like the authors to give reasons why they decided to study this problem directly rather than applying the theory provided previously in those three papers. If they couldn’t, I would like the authors to briefly describe why.

I also have a few other questions and suggestions. In Definition 1 the authors say that a problem is mildly ill-posed if $\kappa_j$ has exponential decay. Do the authors mean polynomial decay? Also in Theorem 1, only at the end of the statement do the authors define what it means for a posterior (or a variational posterior) to contract at a certain rate. I would include a definition of what it means to contract at a certain rate before stating Theorem 1. Moreover, I’m not sure if the notation $S_{++}^m$ used at the bottom of page 4 and the notation $\Pi_u$ used at the top of page 5 are introduced anywhere in the paper before they are used.
reviews.limitations: A limitation of this work concerns its practical usability. For example, the authors’ results hold under the assumption that the model is well-specified. It is hard to expect that such an assumption will be satisfied in real-life applications and for real data sets. I believe that the authors should acknowledge this limitation and perhaps comment on possible extensions to instances of model misspecification.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors propose an inducing-point variational Bayes approximation for linear inverse problems. They study the frequentist properties of the arising variational posterior approximation. Specifically, they prove contraction rates for the variational posterior, both in the mildly and severely ill posed case. Moreover, they illustrate the practical relevance of their results with a number of synthetic data experiments.

Strengths:
The paper is well-written and its objectives and scope are clear. It provides an important contribution. As far as I am aware, frequentist properties of variational posteriors arising as inducing-point approximations in linear inverse problems have not been studied explicitly before. Such properties and contraction rates are useful in that they illustrate how the choice of the prior and the number of inducing points in the approximation impacts the statistical properties of the variational posterior. In that sense, they can provide useful guidance for the user.

Weaknesses:
I am not exactly sure if the comparison to the existing literature provided by the authors is substantial enough – I address this among my questions to the authors.

Questions:
In 2020, three papers were published in the Annals of Statistics providing a comprehensive and general study of the frequentist properties of variational posteriors. Two of those papers are cited by the authors as references [2] and [56]. Another one I would add to this list is “$\alpha$-VARIATIONAL INFERENCE WITH STATISTICAL GUARANTEES” by Yang, Pati, Bhattacharya. I wonder if the authors would explain whether the results of those papers could be applied to the variational GP approximations for linear inverse problems, as considered in the authors’ submitted work. If they could, then I would like the authors to give reasons why they decided to study this problem directly rather than applying the theory provided previously in those three papers. If they couldn’t, I would like the authors to briefly describe why.

I also have a few other questions and suggestions. In Definition 1 the authors say that a problem is mildly ill-posed if $\kappa_j$ has exponential decay. Do the authors mean polynomial decay? Also in Theorem 1, only at the end of the statement do the authors define what it means for a posterior (or a variational posterior) to contract at a certain rate. I would include a definition of what it means to contract at a certain rate before stating Theorem 1. Moreover, I’m not sure if the notation $S_{++}^m$ used at the bottom of page 4 and the notation $\Pi_u$ used at the top of page 5 are introduced anywhere in the paper before they are used.

Limitations:
A limitation of this work concerns its practical usability. For example, the authors’ results hold under the assumption that the model is well-specified. It is hard to expect that such an assumption will be satisfied in real-life applications and for real data sets. I believe that the authors should acknowledge this limitation and perhaps comment on possible extensions to instances of model misspecification.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This manuscript presents a variational Bayes approximation scheme based on inducing points for addressing linear inverse problems. The authors investigate the frequentist behavior of the resulting variational posterior, deriving contraction rates applicable to both mildly and severely ill-posed scenarios. The theoretical findings are complemented by numerical experiments on synthetic datasets that demonstrate practical relevance.

## Strengths

The manuscript is clearly presented with well-articulated goals and scope. It makes a meaningful contribution to the field by offering what appears to be the first explicit analysis of frequentist properties and contraction rates for variational posteriors derived from inducing-point approximations in the context of linear inverse problems. This analysis is valuable because it elucidates how decisions regarding prior specification and the number of inducing points influence the statistical behavior of the variational posterior, thereby offering practitioners principled guidance for implementation choices.

## Weaknesses

The positioning relative to existing work deserves closer examination. While the authors cite relevant literature, the distinction between this work and prior contributions could be articulated more thoroughly.

## Questions and Concerns

Three papers published in the Annals of Statistics in 2020 provide a broad theoretical framework for studying frequentist properties of variational posteriors. The authors cite two of these (references [2] and [56]), but I would also recommend considering "$\alpha$-VARIATIONAL INFERENCE WITH STATISTICAL GUARANTEES" by Yang, Pati, and Bhattacharya. Could the authors clarify whether the general theory developed in these three papers is applicable to their setting? If so, what motivated the choice to develop problem-specific results rather than leveraging the existing general framework? Conversely, if these results do not apply, the authors should explain the technical obstacles that prevent their use.

Additionally, I have several technical questions:

- In Definition 1, the authors characterize mildly ill-posed problems as having eigenvalues with exponential decay. Should this be polynomial decay instead?
- Theorem 1 introduces the notion of posterior contraction at a given rate only in the statement itself. It would improve readability to define this concept explicitly before presenting the theorem.
- The notation $S_{++}^m$ (bottom of page 4) and $\Pi_u$ (top of page 5) appear to be used without prior introduction in the manuscript.

## Limitations

A notable limitation concerns practical applicability. The theoretical guarantees rely on the assumption of correct model specification, a condition rarely satisfied in real-world applications. The authors should explicitly acknowledge this limitation and discuss potential avenues for extending the results to scenarios involving model misspecification.

## Overall Assessment

The paper is technically sound with moderate-to-high impact potential. No significant concerns arise regarding the evaluation methodology, computational resources, reproducibility, or ethical considerations. The work represents a solid contribution despite the areas for improvement noted above.

## Rating

**6: Weak Accept** – Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, or ethical considerations.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors propose an inducing-point variational Bayes approximation for linear inverse problems. They study the frequentist properties of the arising variational posterior approximation. Specifically, they prove contraction rates for the variational posterior, both in the mildly and severely ill posed case. Moreover, they illustrate the practical relevance of their results with a number of synthetic data experiments.

Strengths:
The paper is well-written and its objectives and scope are clear. It provides an important contribution. As far as I am aware, frequentist properties of variational posteriors arising as inducing-point approximations in linear inverse problems have not been studied explicitly before. Such properties and contraction rates are useful in that they illustrate how the choice of the prior and the number of inducing points in the approximation impacts the statistical properties of the variational posterior. In that sense, they can provide useful guidance for the user.

Weaknesses:
I am not exactly sure if the comparison to the existing literature provided by the authors is substantial enough – I address this among my questions to the authors.

Questions:
In 2020, three papers were published in the Annals of Statistics providing a comprehensive and general study of the frequentist properties of variational posteriors. Two of those papers are cited by the authors as references [2] and [56]. Another one I would add to this list is “$\alpha$-VARIATIONAL INFERENCE WITH STATISTICAL GUARANTEES” by Yang, Pati, Bhattacharya. I wonder if the authors would explain whether the results of those papers could be applied to the variational GP approximations for linear inverse problems, as considered in the authors’ submitted work. If they could, then I would like the authors to give reasons why they decided to study this problem directly rather than applying the theory provided previously in those three papers. If they couldn’t, I would like the authors to briefly describe why.

I also have a few other questions and suggestions. In Definition 1 the authors say that a problem is mildly ill-posed if $\kappa_j$ has exponential decay. Do the authors mean polynomial decay? Also in Theorem 1, only at the end of the statement do the authors define what it means for a posterior (or a variational posterior) to contract at a certain rate. I would include a definition of what it means to contract at a certain rate before stating Theorem 1. Moreover, I’m not sure if the notation $S_{++}^m$ used at the bottom of page 4 and the notation $\Pi_u$ used at the top of page 5 are introduced anywhere in the paper before they are used.

Limitations:
A limitation of this work concerns its practical usability. For example, the authors’ results hold under the assumption that the model is well-specified. It is hard to expect that such an assumption will be satisfied in real-life applications and for real data sets. I believe that the authors should acknowledge this limitation and perhaps comment on possible extensions to instances of model misspecification.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a theoretical analysis of the posterior contraction rates for the variational posterior in the context of linear inverse problems. Specifically, the variational posterior is obtained using the sparse Gaussian processes approach, with inducing variables learned through KL divergence minimization. The authors investigate three types of operators: the Volterra operator, the heat equation, and the Radon transform, each exhibiting varying degrees of ill-posedness. The numerical analysis focuses on synthetic toy problems, and the authors demonstrate that in this case, even a small number of inducing points can yield highly accurate results, achieving significant speed-up.
reviews.strengths:
[Originality] I am not up-to-date with theoretical results with frequentist-Bayes analysis, and how far the presented results are based on or deviates from the monograph Nickl, Richard, 2023.  

Nickl, Richard. "Bayesian non-linear statistical inverse problems." (2023).

[Quality] The theoretical results seems sound. 

[Clarity] The paper is well-written; however, the use of jargon limits its accessibility to a narrower audience.

[Significance] The inducing-point of Gaussian processes is widely used, so the theoretical results concerned with it should be of interest to the community working on inverse problems.
reviews.weaknesses:
[Quality] The experimental evaluation is limited to a synthetic toy example only.  

[Significance] The authors motivate the variational Bayes approach for computational reasons. However, recent developments of scalable GP methods can reduce the complexity of exact GP from O(n^3) to O(n^2) and effectively leverage the modern computing hardware such as GPU (Gardner, Jacob, et al.  2018). In this regard, one can afford many more inducing points in practice. 

Gardner, Jacob, et al. "Gpytorch: Blackbox matrix-matrix gaussian process inference with gpu acceleration." *Advances in neural information processing systems* 31 (2018).

The paper is a theory paper, but its practical significance appears to be lacking. It would greatly enhance the paper's value if the authors could at least mention some real-world applications in which the studied inverse problem commonly arises. For instance, discussing the application of the Radon transform in computed tomography (CT) could serve as a relevant and illustrative example.
reviews.questions:
Figure 1 shows that using m=3 is insufficient, while m=6 yields highly accurate results for the studied case, while this is a single case not quite informative. I am wondering whether it is possible to establish a "phase-transition curve" that can inform practitioners about the required number of inducing points for various types of inverse problems and different numbers of measurements. 

If the results are not understood correctly, it might leads to poor choice on the number of inducing points,  and over-or-under estimate of the posterior uncertainty. This could be harmful in mission-critical applications such as medical imaging. How to prevent this happen? 

Could you please further explain why the posterior uncertainty being over-estimated while in traditional variational approximation the uncertainty usually got underestimated? How well is the uncertainty calibrated?
reviews.limitations: The authors do have discussed to what extend the presented results can be extended to nonlinear cases and other types of inverse problems.

Specifically, the variational posterior is obtained using the sparse Gaussian processes approach, with inducing variables learned through KL divergence minimization. The authors investigate three types of operators: the Volterra operator, the heat equation, and the Radon transform, each exhibiting varying degrees of ill-posedness. The numerical analysis focuses on synthetic toy problems, and the authors demonstrate that in this case, even a small number of inducing points can yield highly accurate results, achieving significant speed-up.

[Significance] The authors motivate the variational Bayes approach for computational reasons. However, recent developments of scalable GP methods can reduce the complexity of exact GP from O(n^3) to O(n^2) and effectively leverage the modern computing hardware such as GPU (Gardner, Jacob, et al.  2018). In this regard, one can afford many more inducing points in practice. 

Gardner, Jacob, et al. "Gpytorch: Blackbox matrix-matrix gaussian process inference with gpu acceleration." *Advances in neural information processing systems* 31 (2018).

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This work provides a theoretical investigation of posterior contraction rates for variational posteriors applied to linear inverse problems. The authors employ sparse Gaussian processes with inducing variables optimized via KL divergence minimization. The study examines three distinct operator classes—the Volterra operator, the heat equation, and the Radon transform—which differ in their degree of ill-posedness. Experiments are conducted on synthetic benchmark problems, where the authors show that accurate solutions can be obtained with relatively few inducing points, yielding substantial computational improvements.

## Strengths

[Originality] While I cannot claim complete familiarity with recent theoretical advances in frequentist-Bayesian analysis, the contribution appears novel. However, I would benefit from a clearer delineation of how this work extends or diverges from the foundational results in Nickl's recent monograph (Nickl, Richard. "Bayesian non-linear statistical inverse problems." 2023).

[Quality] The theoretical development appears mathematically rigorous and well-founded.

[Clarity] The manuscript is clearly written overall, though the density of technical terminology may limit accessibility to specialists outside the immediate field.

[Significance] Given the widespread adoption of inducing-point methods in Gaussian process modeling, the theoretical guarantees provided here should be of considerable interest to the inverse problems community.

## Weaknesses

[Quality] The experimental validation relies exclusively on synthetic toy problems, which significantly limits the evidence base for the practical utility of the proposed approach.

[Significance] While the authors justify the variational Bayes framework on computational grounds, it is worth noting that recent advances in scalable Gaussian process inference have reduced computational complexity from O(n³) to O(n²) and enable efficient GPU utilization (Gardner, Jacob, et al. "Gpytorch: Blackbox matrix-matrix gaussian process inference with gpu acceleration." *Advances in neural information processing systems* 31, 2018). This suggests that practitioners may have the capacity to employ substantially more inducing points than previously feasible.

As a primarily theoretical contribution, the paper's real-world applicability remains somewhat unclear. The work would be considerably strengthened by discussing concrete application domains where the studied inverse problems naturally arise. For example, the Radon transform's role in computed tomography (CT) imaging would provide a concrete and relevant use case that illustrates the practical relevance of the theoretical findings.

## Questions and Concerns

Figure 1 presents results for a single scenario, showing that m=3 inducing points prove inadequate while m=6 provides accurate solutions. However, this limited evidence raises important practical questions: Can the authors develop guidance in the form of a "phase-transition curve" or similar tool that would help practitioners determine the appropriate number of inducing points across different inverse problem types and varying measurement regimes?

A related concern involves the potential for misapplication of these results. Incorrect choices regarding the number of inducing points could lead to systematic miscalibration of posterior uncertainty estimates, either overestimating or underestimating credible intervals. In safety-critical domains such as medical imaging, such errors could have serious consequences. What safeguards or diagnostic tools do the authors recommend to mitigate this risk?

Furthermore, the observation that the posterior uncertainty is overestimated warrants clarification. This finding appears counterintuitive given that standard variational approximations typically underestimate uncertainty. The authors should provide additional explanation for this phenomenon and offer assessment of the overall calibration quality of the uncertainty estimates.

## Limitations

The authors acknowledge but do not extensively explore the scope of their results. Greater discussion of how the theoretical framework might extend to nonlinear inverse problems and broader classes of operators would enhance the contribution.

## Recommendation

**6: Weak Accept** — The paper presents technically sound theoretical results with moderate-to-high potential impact. The mathematical development is solid and the core findings appear reliable. However, the gap between theory and practice, combined with limited experimental validation and unresolved questions about practical guidance for practitioners, prevents a stronger recommendation. The work makes a meaningful contribution to the theoretical understanding of variational inference in inverse problems, but would benefit from addressing the practical considerations outlined above.

Summary:
This paper analyzes posterior contraction rates for the inducing variables variational Bayes approximation for Gaussian process. In particular, mildly and ill-posed inverse problem settings, which are characterized by profiles of eigenvalues of measurement operators, are considered. For the both cases, the posterior contract rates are assessed theoretically. The usefulness of the results are shown for applications to three example problems.

Strengths:
The posterior contract rates are assessed analytically for the inducing variable variational Bayes approximation of Gaussian process. This is useful to determine the optimal number of inducing variables in the approximation.

Weaknesses:
The analysis relies on the assumption that the functional space where the target function belongs is known. However, there are many practical situations where such an assumption does not hold. The analysis is not applicable for such situations.

Questions:
What happens if the target function does not belong to the assumed functional space?

Limitations:
Yes.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This work investigates convergence behavior of posterior distributions when employing inducing variable variational Bayes approximations for Gaussian processes, with particular focus on mildly and ill-posed inverse problem contexts. The analysis distinguishes between these two problem classes based on eigenvalue decay characteristics of the measurement operators involved. Theoretical posterior contraction rates are derived for both settings, and the practical relevance of these findings is demonstrated through three illustrative applications.

## Strengths

A key contribution of this paper is the provision of analytical characterizations of posterior contraction rates specifically for the inducing variable variational Bayes approximation framework applied to Gaussian processes. This theoretical analysis offers valuable guidance for practitioners seeking to determine the appropriate number of inducing variables required in their approximations.

## Weaknesses

The theoretical framework presented in this work fundamentally depends on prior knowledge of the functional space inhabited by the target function. This represents a significant limitation, as numerous real-world applications involve settings where the true functional space is unknown or uncertain. Consequently, the applicability of the proposed analysis is restricted to scenarios where such strong prior knowledge is available, limiting its generalizability across diverse practical domains.

## Questions

How does the methodology perform when the target function belongs to a different functional space than the one assumed in the analysis?

## Limitations

Yes, the work acknowledges notable limitations.

## Rating

5: Borderline accept: Technically sound contribution where the merits of acceptance are marginally stronger than the reasons for rejection, such as restricted scope of empirical validation. This rating should be employed judiciously.

## Decision

**Accept** - The paper merits publication despite its constraints, as the technical quality and theoretical insights provided outweigh the acknowledged limitations.

# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This work investigates posterior contraction rates for Variational Gaussian Processes applied to mildly and severely ill-posed linear inverse problems. The key theoretical contribution demonstrates that, under appropriate conditions, the variational posterior achieves contraction rates equivalent to those of the true posterior. Additionally, the authors establish that a sufficient number of inducing points (selected in the spectral domain) is sufficient to ensure these conditions are satisfied. The paper exemplifies these findings through concrete case studies: two mildly ill-posed scenarios (Volterra operators and Radon transforms) and one severely ill-posed scenario (heat equation), where explicit bounds on the required number of inducing points for optimal contraction are derived.

## Strengths

The theoretical investigation of posterior contraction rates substantially advances our knowledge of how Variational GP methods behave in the context of linear inverse problems.

The manuscript demonstrates clear and logical organization throughout.

The core contributions are articulated in a compelling and transparent manner.

## Weaknesses

The empirical evaluation is notably sparse in the main body of the paper, with the majority of experimental results relegated to supplementary materials.

The paper appears better suited to journal publication rather than a conference venue. The highly technical nature of the work demands extensive scrutiny, which is challenging to accommodate within typical conference review constraints.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. Enhance the visual presentation of Figure 1, particularly by enlarging the legend to improve legibility.

## Treatment of Limitations

The authors adequately acknowledge and discuss the limitations of their work.

## Overall Assessment and Recommendation

This is a technically competent contribution where the theoretical merits and advances justify acceptance despite certain limitations. The primary concern—namely, the limited experimental validation—is offset by the rigor and novelty of the theoretical analysis. The paper represents solid work that advances understanding of variational methods for inverse problems, though broader empirical validation would strengthen the contribution.

**Rating: 5 (Borderline Accept)** — A technically sound paper in which the reasons for acceptance outweigh those for rejection, such as the restricted experimental scope. This rating is applied judiciously.

title: Localized Symbolic Knowledge Distillation for Visual Commonsense Models

reviews.summary: This work presents a localized visual common sense model that can support region-level inputs for knowledge reasoning. Specifically, the proposed methods prompts large language models to collect commonsense knowledge from global image descriptions and local descriptions. A critic classifier trained over a small number of annotation data is used to select high-quality examples. The distilled localized common-sense corpus can be further used for fine-tuning vision-language models. The experimental results demonstrate improvement over baselines in the zero-shot setting.
reviews.strengths:
a). Strong motivation; supporting region-level references and reasoning is important for many applications.

b). Technical sound;  provide detailed and clear explanation of each component; experiments and ablation studies are comprehensive. 

c). Thorough evaluation; have human evaluations comparing zero-shot performance of localized reasoning with generative models.
reviews.weaknesses:
a). A new way of distilling vision-language knowledge but no framework-level innovation compared with text only symbolic distillation. 

b). The effectiveness of supervised critic: as the validity of instance annotation is only 45% and the trained classifier can only achieve 0.66 on F1 score. It is unclear whether the filtering process can be effective in selecting high-quality examples.
reviews.questions:
a). Can the collected 1M instances of localized commonsense knowledge be used for other relevant vision-language tasks? Will the knowledge further benefit those tasks? 

b). In the Section of “effect of filtering”, the comparison with LLaVa datasets seems unfair since the number of LLaVa is fixed. What is the number of training data for LLaVa?
reviews.limitations: n.a.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This work presents a localized visual common sense model that can support region-level inputs for knowledge reasoning. Specifically, the proposed methods prompts large language models to collect commonsense knowledge from global image descriptions and local descriptions. A critic classifier trained over a small number of annotation data is used to select high-quality examples. The distilled localized common-sense corpus can be further used for fine-tuning vision-language models. The experimental results demonstrate improvement over baselines in the zero-shot setting.

Strengths:
a). Strong motivation; supporting region-level references and reasoning is important for many applications.

b). Technical sound;  provide detailed and clear explanation of each component; experiments and ablation studies are comprehensive. 

c). Thorough evaluation; have human evaluations comparing zero-shot performance of localized reasoning with generative models.

Weaknesses:
a). A new way of distilling vision-language knowledge but no framework-level innovation compared with text only symbolic distillation. 

b). The effectiveness of supervised critic: as the validity of instance annotation is only 45% and the trained classifier can only achieve 0.66 on F1 score. It is unclear whether the filtering process can be effective in selecting high-quality examples.

Questions:
a). Can the collected 1M instances of localized commonsense knowledge be used for other relevant vision-language tasks? Will the knowledge further benefit those tasks? 

b). In the Section of “effect of filtering”, the comparison with LLaVa datasets seems unfair since the number of LLaVa is fixed. What is the number of training data for LLaVa?

Limitations:
n.a.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This paper introduces a method for developing region-aware visual commonsense reasoning by leveraging large language models to extract commonsense knowledge from both image-level and region-level descriptions. The authors employ a learned critic classifier, trained on limited annotated data, to filter high-quality instances. The resulting localized commonsense knowledge base can subsequently be used to fine-tune vision-language models, with experimental validation showing improvements over baseline approaches in zero-shot evaluation scenarios.

## Strengths

a) **Well-motivated research direction**: The focus on enabling region-level understanding and reasoning addresses a genuine gap in current systems and has clear relevance for practical applications.

b) **Solid technical execution**: The paper provides clear, detailed descriptions of all proposed components and includes comprehensive experimental validation alongside thorough ablation studies.

c) **Rigorous empirical assessment**: The evaluation incorporates human studies that directly compare zero-shot localized reasoning capabilities against existing generative models, strengthening the validity of the claims.

## Weaknesses

a) **Limited conceptual novelty**: While the application to vision-language models is useful, the underlying distillation approach represents an incremental extension of existing text-based symbolic distillation methods without introducing fundamentally new framework-level insights.

b) **Questionable filtering reliability**: The critic classifier exhibits concerning performance characteristics—with only 45% validity in the instance annotations and an F1 score of 0.66—raising substantial doubts about whether the filtering mechanism can reliably identify high-quality examples for downstream use.

## Questions

a) **Generalization potential**: Can the 1M localized commonsense instances collected in this work transfer to other related vision-language tasks? Would the knowledge provide meaningful benefits when applied to these alternative domains?

b) **Dataset comparison fairness**: In the filtering effectiveness analysis, the comparison with LLaVa appears potentially problematic given that LLaVa's dataset size is held constant. Could the authors clarify the actual training data volume used for LLaVa in their experiments?

## Limitations

Not addressed in original review.

## Overall Assessment and Recommendation

**Rating: 6 - Weak Accept**

The paper presents technically sound work with moderate-to-high practical impact. The experimental methodology is rigorous, with no significant issues concerning evaluation procedures, resource availability, reproducibility, or ethical dimensions. While the conceptual contributions are somewhat incremental and questions remain about the reliability of the filtering mechanism, the work makes a sufficient contribution to warrant publication.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This work presents a localized visual common sense model that can support region-level inputs for knowledge reasoning. Specifically, the proposed methods prompts large language models to collect commonsense knowledge from global image descriptions and local descriptions. A critic classifier trained over a small number of annotation data is used to select high-quality examples. The distilled localized common-sense corpus can be further used for fine-tuning vision-language models. The experimental results demonstrate improvement over baselines in the zero-shot setting.

Strengths:
a). Strong motivation; supporting region-level references and reasoning is important for many applications.

b). Technical sound;  provide detailed and clear explanation of each component; experiments and ablation studies are comprehensive. 

c). Thorough evaluation; have human evaluations comparing zero-shot performance of localized reasoning with generative models.

Weaknesses:
a). A new way of distilling vision-language knowledge but no framework-level innovation compared with text only symbolic distillation. 

b). The effectiveness of supervised critic: as the validity of instance annotation is only 45% and the trained classifier can only achieve 0.66 on F1 score. It is unclear whether the filtering process can be effective in selecting high-quality examples.

Questions:
a). Can the collected 1M instances of localized commonsense knowledge be used for other relevant vision-language tasks? Will the knowledge further benefit those tasks? 

b). In the Section of “effect of filtering”, the comparison with LLaVa datasets seems unfair since the number of LLaVa is fixed. What is the number of training data for LLaVa?

Limitations:
n.a.

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: In this paper, authors argue that for multimodal LLMs, the previous input formulation is too rigid: either needs to specify the region models should focus on, or brings a verbose object description to refer to the region. A more natural referring expression strategy can help models better understand where the model should pay more attention to and the intent of the input questions. The proposed approach, called Localized Symbolic Knowledge Distillation (LSKD), aims to provide more natural referring expressions. It involves providing literal descriptions of images to a large language model and allowing the model to connect these descriptions with a holistic understanding of the scene. In addition, localized references to specific regions within the image are provided to encourage the model to generate commonsense inferences about those regions. This method effectively distills the large language model's capacity for global and local scene understanding. They demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach by achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on localized visual reasoning benchmarks and conducting human evaluations.
reviews.strengths:
Novel research question to distill visual commonsense: Distilling visual commonsense is an interesting question that previous works under-explore. The distilled visual commonsense could provide very useful information for understanding complex situations covered in datasets like VCR, which will make the reasoning more grounded and reliable. Also, this is the first work about automatically acquiring visual commonsense to my best knowledge. 

Comprehensive experimental results on downstream tasks: I'm very glad to see the distilled knowledge could further help base models on downstream tasks like VCR, Sherlock and VisualCOMET. Also, the small models can even outperform ChatGPT with the help of LSKD when describing the useful information in localized reasoning process. Besides, authors design experiments to examine the effect of steps in LSKD generation methods, which further make the argument stronger.
reviews.weaknesses:
Lack of comparison with Sherlock and VisualCOMET: Although the current experiments support that LSKD is helpful for downstream tasks, I'm still a bit confused if LSKD is better than Sherlock and VisualCOMET knowledge bases. These two datasets are both large-scale and of great quality. It's better to compare with competitive resources, instead of just showing that considering external knowledge will help. 

Comparing VL models and ChatGPT is unfair: Although ChatGPT is prompted with verbalizers, it still can only accept text information, which may ignore many visual information in the images. Especially for datasets like VCR, answering questions may need a very careful observation to some detailed places, which is what VL models like BLIP-2 is better at.

Presentation in introduction section is confusing: I don't quite understand the relation between the first and second half of the introduction section. For the first half, I think the authors are trying to say a better referring expression is important. But in the second half (which is the core part of the paper), they just focus on how to distill visual commonsense knowledge. They are a bit connected because both parts mention localized reference, but it has very loose connection with discussion about the quality of referring expression.
reviews.questions:
1. Is there any way to apply this method to the datasets with no bounding box annotations? That will further improve the scalability of this method.
2. Is there any study to explore the correlation between the ration of error training instances and the downstream task performance?
3. Could you also apply LSKD to models like CLIP? It seems that in Table 2, they just use BLIP-2 ViT-G to show the effectiveness of LSKD.
4. More qualitative error analysis is welcome in the modified version.
reviews.limitations: The limitations about introducing errors in the intermediate steps are reasonable.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, authors argue that for multimodal LLMs, the previous input formulation is too rigid: either needs to specify the region models should focus on, or brings a verbose object description to refer to the region. A more natural referring expression strategy can help models better understand where the model should pay more attention to and the intent of the input questions. The proposed approach, called Localized Symbolic Knowledge Distillation (LSKD), aims to provide more natural referring expressions. It involves providing literal descriptions of images to a large language model and allowing the model to connect these descriptions with a holistic understanding of the scene. In addition, localized references to specific regions within the image are provided to encourage the model to generate commonsense inferences about those regions. This method effectively distills the large language model's capacity for global and local scene understanding. They demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach by achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on localized visual reasoning benchmarks and conducting human evaluations.

Strengths:
Novel research question to distill visual commonsense: Distilling visual commonsense is an interesting question that previous works under-explore. The distilled visual commonsense could provide very useful information for understanding complex situations covered in datasets like VCR, which will make the reasoning more grounded and reliable. Also, this is the first work about automatically acquiring visual commonsense to my best knowledge. 

Comprehensive experimental results on downstream tasks: I'm very glad to see the distilled knowledge could further help base models on downstream tasks like VCR, Sherlock and VisualCOMET. Also, the small models can even outperform ChatGPT with the help of LSKD when describing the useful information in localized reasoning process. Besides, authors design experiments to examine the effect of steps in LSKD generation methods, which further make the argument stronger.

Weaknesses:
Lack of comparison with Sherlock and VisualCOMET: Although the current experiments support that LSKD is helpful for downstream tasks, I'm still a bit confused if LSKD is better than Sherlock and VisualCOMET knowledge bases. These two datasets are both large-scale and of great quality. It's better to compare with competitive resources, instead of just showing that considering external knowledge will help. 

Comparing VL models and ChatGPT is unfair: Although ChatGPT is prompted with verbalizers, it still can only accept text information, which may ignore many visual information in the images. Especially for datasets like VCR, answering questions may need a very careful observation to some detailed places, which is what VL models like BLIP-2 is better at.

Presentation in introduction section is confusing: I don't quite understand the relation between the first and second half of the introduction section. For the first half, I think the authors are trying to say a better referring expression is important. But in the second half (which is the core part of the paper), they just focus on how to distill visual commonsense knowledge. They are a bit connected because both parts mention localized reference, but it has very loose connection with discussion about the quality of referring expression.

Questions:
1. Is there any way to apply this method to the datasets with no bounding box annotations? That will further improve the scalability of this method.
2. Is there any study to explore the correlation between the ration of error training instances and the downstream task performance?
3. Could you also apply LSKD to models like CLIP? It seems that in Table 2, they just use BLIP-2 ViT-G to show the effectiveness of LSKD.
4. More qualitative error analysis is welcome in the modified version.

Limitations:
The limitations about introducing errors in the intermediate steps are reasonable.

Rating:
8: Strong Accept: Technically strong paper, with novel ideas, excellent impact on at least one area, or high-to-excellent impact on multiple areas, with excellent evaluation, resources, and reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This paper introduces a novel framework called Localized Symbolic Knowledge Distillation (LSKD) to address limitations in how multimodal language models process spatial information. The authors argue that existing approaches to specifying image regions are suboptimal—either requiring explicit region annotations or relying on verbose object descriptions. The proposed method leverages more intuitive referring expressions by combining holistic image descriptions with localized region-specific commentary, enabling models to generate contextually-grounded commonsense inferences. The authors validate their approach through zero-shot evaluation on localized visual reasoning benchmarks and supplementary human evaluation studies.

## Strengths

**Novel contribution to visual commonsense acquisition**: The paper tackles an underexplored problem of automatically extracting visual commonsense knowledge, which represents a meaningful advancement in the field. The distilled commonsense has clear applicability to complex reasoning scenarios such as those found in VCR, where grounded and interpretable reasoning is essential. To the best of my knowledge, this is the first systematic approach to automated visual commonsense acquisition, making it a valuable contribution.

**Thorough empirical validation across multiple downstream applications**: The authors convincingly demonstrate that LSKD-distilled knowledge generalizes well to several challenging tasks including VCR, Sherlock, and VisualCOMET. Notably, the results show that smaller models enhanced with LSKD can rival or exceed ChatGPT's performance in localized reasoning scenarios. The ablation studies examining individual components of the LSKD pipeline strengthen the empirical claims and provide useful insights into which design choices matter most.

## Weaknesses

**Insufficient direct comparison with existing knowledge resources**: While the paper shows that incorporating external knowledge benefits downstream performance, there is insufficient analysis comparing LSKD against established high-quality resources like Sherlock and VisualCOMET. Since these are large-scale, curated datasets themselves, a more rigorous comparative evaluation would be needed to establish whether LSKD offers meaningful advantages over these competitive baselines, rather than simply demonstrating that external knowledge helps in general.

**Methodological concerns regarding ChatGPT comparisons**: The comparison between vision-language models and ChatGPT appears somewhat problematic. ChatGPT's text-only interface fundamentally limits its access to visual information, potentially biasing results in favor of multimodal models. This is particularly relevant for datasets like VCR, where fine-grained visual details and careful observation are critical—precisely where vision-language models like BLIP-2 have inherent advantages. The comparison thus conflates architectural differences with the actual benefits of LSKD.

**Structural clarity issues in the introduction**: The introduction's narrative flow is somewhat disjointed. The initial discussion emphasizes the importance of improved referring expressions, while the subsequent sections pivot to focus primarily on visual commonsense distillation. Although both concepts involve localized references, the connection between these two threads feels loose and underdeveloped, potentially confusing readers about the paper's core contribution.

## Questions and Suggestions

1. How would this method scale to datasets lacking bounding box annotations? Addressing this limitation would significantly broaden the applicability of the approach.

2. Have the authors conducted analysis examining how the proportion of erroneous instances in the distillation process correlates with downstream task performance? Understanding this relationship would be valuable.

3. Could the authors demonstrate LSKD's effectiveness beyond BLIP-2 ViT-G? Extending Table 2 to include applications to other architectures like CLIP would strengthen claims about generalizability.

4. The paper would benefit from more extensive qualitative error analysis, including concrete examples of failure cases and their underlying causes.

## Limitations Discussion

The authors' acknowledgment of potential error propagation in intermediate pipeline steps is appropriate and reasonable.

## Recommendation

This paper presents a technically sound contribution with novel ideas and strong empirical results across multiple evaluation settings. The core concept of automatically distilling visual commonsense is both original and impactful, and the experimental work is comprehensive. While the paper has areas for improvement—particularly in comparative analysis and methodological clarity—these issues do not substantially undermine the overall contribution. The work demonstrates excellent evaluation rigor and reproducibility, with clear practical benefits for the multimodal learning community.

**Rating: 8 - Strong Accept**

This is a technically strong paper with novel ideas and high-to-excellent impact across multiple application areas, supported by comprehensive evaluation, solid experimental resources, and no unaddressed ethical concerns.

reviews.summary: This paper aims to build a instruction following model for **localized** visual commonsense reasoning. The work differs from existing works in that it is able to reason about localized image regions with boxes, without using complicated referring expressions. The paper first collects data by distilling LLMs, i.e., prompting GPT using verbalized image descriptions, which is a concatenation of 3 descriptors: global, local, and QA pairs. Then, the data collected in the first stage is filtered by a critic filter trained using human annotations. Finally, the filtered data is used to train a BLIP-2 model. Experiments are done on 3 datasets for the localized visual reasoning task, showing the performance of the proposed method.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper is clearly motivated and the problem studied is well described.
- The dataset collection method is well designed. Using (global, local, QA) descriptors to prompt LLM in order to generate question, answer and rationales.
- The effectiveness of the critic filtering is well-analyzed and clearly shown in Fig 3.
- The paper is well written and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
My major concern is about experiments. The current results may not be very extensive to show the effectiveness of the proposed method.
- Box size is an important factor, since the task is localized reasoning. How does the model perform on large and small objects? How does this compare to other methods? First, it would be helpful to describe the distribution of the bbox sizes in the generated data. Second, in the final evaluation, it is also helpful to study the model performance change wrt different box sizes.
- three descriptors (global, local, QA) are used to prompt LLMs. Is there a study to show the contribution of each of the three? Since QA is not as widely used as the other two, additional discussions/results showing its effectiveness would be good.
- Very limited baselines are compared with. In table 2, only CLIP is compared. In table 3, only ChatGPT. More baselines should be considered. For example, for tab 2, while zero-shot baselines are hard to find, it is still beneficial to show some non zero-shot methods (methods on the leaderboard of these datasets). Although the model performance is not directly comparable, it can give readers some sense of how well the model performs.
- (potential extension) While the contribution on the data collection/distillation part is strong, the contribution on the model part is weak. Basically, BLIP-2 is directly used and trained on the collected data. Could there be some model design specific for handling “localized” information? Moreover, the “localized” reasoning in the current paper is constrained to color-coded bbox, while more general forms like free-form masks can be a further extension.
- The work is in principle similar to LLaVA, extending it to be “localized”. Could the authors discuss the difference with LLaVA in more detail?
- The title suggests “symbolic”, how is this work related to “symbolic”? I feel this is mis-leading.
- some typos: L168 QA-MSE, L146 QAR is not defined.
reviews.questions: See weakness.
reviews.limitations: N/A.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review

## Summary

This paper presents an approach for training a visual instruction-following model capable of performing localized visual commonsense reasoning over specific image regions demarcated by bounding boxes, without relying on complex referring expressions. The methodology consists of three stages: (1) data generation via LLM distillation by prompting GPT with concatenated image descriptions (global context, local details, and QA pairs), (2) quality filtering using a critic model trained on human annotations, and (3) fine-tuning a BLIP-2 model on the curated dataset. The approach is evaluated across three benchmarks designed for localized visual reasoning tasks.

## Strengths

- The paper is well-motivated with a clearly articulated problem statement that is easy to understand.
- The data collection strategy is thoughtfully designed, leveraging a three-component descriptor framework (global, local, QA) to effectively prompt LLMs for generating questions, answers, and reasoning traces.
- The utility and impact of the critic-based filtering mechanism is convincingly demonstrated through thorough analysis and visualization presented in Figure 3.
- The manuscript is clearly written with good organization that facilitates readability and comprehension.

## Weaknesses

The primary limitation concerns the scope and depth of the experimental evaluation, which may be insufficient to fully substantiate the method's effectiveness.

- **Object size analysis**: Given that the task centers on localized reasoning, bounding box scale is a critical factor. The paper lacks investigation into model performance across objects of varying sizes. This should include: (1) characterization of the bbox size distribution in the generated dataset, and (2) performance analysis stratified by box size categories in the evaluation phase, along with comparative results against other methods.

- **Component ablation**: The approach combines three descriptors (global, local, QA) for LLM prompting, yet no ablation study quantifies each component's contribution. Since QA is less conventional than the other two descriptors, explicit evidence demonstrating its value through additional experiments or analysis would strengthen the work.

- **Limited comparative analysis**: The baseline comparisons are sparse—only CLIP is included in Table 2 and ChatGPT in Table 3. The evaluation would benefit from a more comprehensive set of baselines. While zero-shot comparisons may be challenging to find, incorporating non-zero-shot methods from existing dataset leaderboards would provide valuable context for interpreting the results, even if direct performance comparison is not feasible.

- **Model architecture contribution**: While the data collection and distillation components represent solid contributions, the modeling contribution is limited. The approach essentially applies BLIP-2 directly to the curated dataset without architectural innovations tailored for localized information processing. Additionally, the current treatment of "localized" reasoning is restricted to color-coded bounding boxes; extending to more flexible representations such as free-form masks would enhance the generality of the approach.

- **Relationship to prior work**: The paper bears conceptual similarity to LLaVA but extends it to the localized setting. A more detailed discussion clarifying the specific differences and relative contributions compared to LLaVA would be valuable.

- **Terminology clarity**: The title references "symbolic" reasoning, but the connection to symbolic methods is unclear and potentially misleading. This terminology should either be justified with explicit connections or reconsidered.

- **Technical issues**: Several minor issues require correction—undefined notation (QAR at L146), unclear abbreviations (QA-MSE at L168), and general proofreading.

## Questions

The concerns raised above would benefit from author responses and additional analysis.

## Limitations

Not applicable.

## Recommendation

**5: Borderline accept** - This paper presents technically sound work where the strengths in problem formulation and data collection methodology outweigh the experimental limitations. However, the evaluation scope—particularly regarding ablation studies, baseline comparisons, and analysis of performance across object scales—falls short of being comprehensive. The limited novelty in the modeling component further tempers the contribution. With revisions addressing the experimental gaps, this work would constitute a solid contribution to the field.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a framework that can generate global, local, and dynamic descriptions. ChatGPT can generate question-and-answer pairs containing specific regions or descriptive phrases with the proposed tricks. A critic model is trained to filter the generated data. Finally, the localized corpus is used to fine-tune the vision language model.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper proposes a way to acquire localized commonsense knowledge data and enable the vision and language model to answer localized visual commonsense questions. The workload of this work is impressive.

Summary:
This paper proposes a framework that can generate global, local, and dynamic descriptions. ChatGPT can generate question-and-answer pairs containing specific regions or descriptive phrases with the proposed tricks. A critic model is trained to filter the generated data. Finally, the localized corpus is used to fine-tune the vision language model.

Strengths:
1. This paper proposes a way to acquire localized commonsense knowledge data and enable the vision and language model to answer localized visual commonsense questions. The workload of this work is impressive.

2. The fine-tuned vision language model achieves promising zero-shot performance for three localized visual reasoning tasks.

Weaknesses:
1. The proposed framework is more like engineering work. The proposed method to generate localized data pairs is straightforward, and the way to enable the local-level question-answering ability is not novel, limiting its scientific contribution.

2. There are too many tricks, including filtering by critic model and prompts for acquiring question-answering pairs, which decreases the reproducibility and robustness of the whole pipeline. Furthermore, the selection of the vision-language model and large language model, and hyperparameters for each model in each step increase the complexity of the proposed pipeline, and the final vision-language model may be sensitive to the generated data, which makes it hard to iterative improve the final model's performance by improving any step in this pipeline.

Questions:
This paper proposes an impressive engineering framework. It would be better to analyze the impact of the quality and amount of the generated data on the final performance of the visual language model.

Limitations:
I did not find a potential negative societal impact of this work.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

The paper introduces an approach for generating multi-level descriptions (global, local, and dynamic) by leveraging ChatGPT to produce question-answer pairs focused on specific spatial regions or descriptive elements through targeted prompting strategies. A learned critic model is subsequently employed to curate the generated dataset. The resulting localized corpus is then utilized to adapt a vision-language model through fine-tuning.

## Strengths

1. The paper addresses an important problem by developing a methodology to extract localized commonsense knowledge and equip vision-language models with the capacity to perform localized visual commonsense reasoning. The effort invested in executing this work is substantial and noteworthy.

2. The resulting fine-tuned vision-language model demonstrates encouraging zero-shot generalization capabilities across three distinct localized visual reasoning benchmarks.

## Weaknesses

1. The overall contribution reads primarily as an engineering effort rather than a research contribution with significant scientific novelty. The approach to generating localized data pairs is relatively straightforward, and the methodology for imparting local-level question-answering capabilities lacks innovation, which constrains the scientific impact of the work.

2. The framework relies on numerous design choices and heuristics—including critic-based filtering and various prompting strategies for generating question-answer pairs—that substantially compromise reproducibility and pipeline robustness. Additionally, the numerous hyperparameter decisions required across different models and stages, combined with the selection of specific vision-language and large language model variants, introduce considerable complexity. This raises concerns that the final vision-language model may exhibit sensitivity to the particular generated dataset, making it challenging to iteratively enhance performance by refining individual pipeline components.

## Questions and Suggestions

The paper presents a technically competent engineering solution. It would strengthen the contribution to systematically investigate how variations in the quality and quantity of generated training data influence the downstream performance of the resulting vision-language model.

## Limitations Discussion

No significant negative societal impacts were identified in this work.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 5 (Borderline accept)** - This is a technically competent paper where the merits of acceptance marginally outweigh the concerns, though the evaluation could be more comprehensive. This rating should be applied judiciously.

reviews.summary: The paper introduces Localized Visual Commonsense models that enhance vision-language (VL) models by allowing users to specify specific regions within images. The authors train their model by collecting commonsense knowledge from a large language model (LLM) using global literal image descriptions and automatically generating local literal region descriptions from VL models. They also use a critic model to select high-quality examples. The results show that training on this localized commonsense corpus improves the performance of VL models of passing generated referring expressions to an LLM.
reviews.strengths:
+ The paper is well-motivated. The introduction of Localized Visual Commonsense models represents a novel solution to build a more general multi-modal model.

+ The constructed data is of large scale, which contains 1M samples over 250K images. 

+ The extensive experiments show that the proposed dataset can support model to perform better zero-shot inference.
reviews.weaknesses:
- How does this Dataset reflect knowledge? Since ChatGPT's input is based on factual descriptions of images, how can we ensure the questions generated by ChatGPT are related to external knowledge? On the other hand, how do the authors define what are knowledge-related questions?

- For some knowledge-related questions, it seems that ChatGPT needs to guess the state of the subject in the image. Could this process result in hallucination issues? I notice that there's a critic filtering in the method, which rates the results by training on annotated data. What types of patterns is this model expected to learn, and under what circumstances does it consider the generated data to be inadequate? From Section 2.3, it seems to be judging visual correctness, but how can the correctness of the generated knowledge be determined?

For example, in Fig1, the model assumes that [0] and [2] are teaching [1]. This is a guess made by ChatGPT, as no similar descriptions are generated by the visual models, indicating that they cannot capture this information. So how would the critic model determine if this kind of guess is based on reality or is just a hallucination?
reviews.questions: My major concern with this work is how to guarantee the dataset quality since there are a lot of automatic generation methods in dataset construction. So I would like to hear more illustrations about that.

Summary:
The paper introduces Localized Visual Commonsense models that enhance vision-language (VL) models by allowing users to specify specific regions within images. The authors train their model by collecting commonsense knowledge from a large language model (LLM) using global literal image descriptions and automatically generating local literal region descriptions from VL models. They also use a critic model to select high-quality examples. The results show that training on this localized commonsense corpus improves the performance of VL models of passing generated referring expressions to an LLM.

Strengths:
+ The paper is well-motivated. The introduction of Localized Visual Commonsense models represents a novel solution to build a more general multi-modal model.

+ The constructed data is of large scale, which contains 1M samples over 250K images. 

+ The extensive experiments show that the proposed dataset can support model to perform better zero-shot inference.

Weaknesses:
- How does this Dataset reflect knowledge? Since ChatGPT's input is based on factual descriptions of images, how can we ensure the questions generated by ChatGPT are related to external knowledge? On the other hand, how do the authors define what are knowledge-related questions?

- For some knowledge-related questions, it seems that ChatGPT needs to guess the state of the subject in the image. Could this process result in hallucination issues? I notice that there's a critic filtering in the method, which rates the results by training on annotated data. What types of patterns is this model expected to learn, and under what circumstances does it consider the generated data to be inadequate? From Section 2.3, it seems to be judging visual correctness, but how can the correctness of the generated knowledge be determined?

For example, in Fig1, the model assumes that [0] and [2] are teaching [1]. This is a guess made by ChatGPT, as no similar descriptions are generated by the visual models, indicating that they cannot capture this information. So how would the critic model determine if this kind of guess is based on reality or is just a hallucination?

Questions:
My major concern with this work is how to guarantee the dataset quality since there are a lot of automatic generation methods in dataset construction. So I would like to hear more illustrations about that.

5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This work proposes Localized Visual Commonsense models that extend existing vision-language frameworks by enabling fine-grained specification of image regions of interest. The approach involves leveraging an LLM to generate commonsense knowledge grounded in global image descriptions, automatically deriving region-level descriptions through existing VL models, and employing a filtering mechanism to identify high-quality instances. The empirical evaluation demonstrates that models trained on this localized commonsense dataset achieve improved zero-shot performance when augmented with region-specific information passed to an LLM.

## Strengths

+ The work addresses a genuine limitation in current vision-language models and proposes a well-motivated direction toward more spatially-aware commonsense understanding.

+ The dataset construction effort is substantial, yielding 1M instances spanning 250K images, representing a significant resource for the community.

+ The empirical evaluation is comprehensive and demonstrates consistent improvements in zero-shot inference performance across the proposed benchmarks.

## Weaknesses

- A critical question concerns the nature of what constitutes "knowledge" in the constructed dataset. Given that ChatGPT's inputs consist of factual image descriptions, the mechanisms ensuring that generated outputs represent genuine commonsense knowledge rather than description paraphrasing remain unclear. Furthermore, the paper lacks explicit criteria for distinguishing knowledge-bearing questions from purely descriptive ones.

- The reliance on ChatGPT for inferring spatial relationships and states introduces potential hallucination risks. While the authors incorporate a critic model for quality control, the review process itself raises concerns. Specifically, what representational patterns does this critic learn from its training data, and what constitutes its decision boundary for rejecting samples? Section 2.3 suggests the critic evaluates visual correctness, yet the methodology for validating the correctness of inferred commonsense knowledge remains unspecified.

For instance, the example in Figure 1 showing ChatGPT inferring a teaching relationship between regions [0], [1], and [2] exemplifies this concern. Since the visual models do not capture such relationships, this represents an inference or guess by ChatGPT. The question then becomes: how does the critic model distinguish between valid commonsense inferences grounded in visual evidence and spurious hallucinations lacking factual basis?

## Questions

The primary concern centers on dataset quality assurance given the extensive use of automated generation throughout the construction pipeline. The authors should provide clearer exposition regarding quality control mechanisms and validation strategies.

## Rating

**5: Borderline accept** - The paper presents technically sound methodology and demonstrates empirical improvements, with the strengths of the contribution and experimental validation outweighing the limitations; however, unresolved concerns regarding data quality and hallucination mitigation prevent a stronger recommendation.

Summary:
The paper introduces Localized Visual Commonsense models that enhance vision-language (VL) models by allowing users to specify specific regions within images. The authors train their model by collecting commonsense knowledge from a large language model (LLM) using global literal image descriptions and automatically generating local literal region descriptions from VL models. They also use a critic model to select high-quality examples. The results show that training on this localized commonsense corpus improves the performance of VL models of passing generated referring expressions to an LLM.

Strengths:
+ The paper is well-motivated. The introduction of Localized Visual Commonsense models represents a novel solution to build a more general multi-modal model.

+ The constructed data is of large scale, which contains 1M samples over 250K images. 

+ The extensive experiments show that the proposed dataset can support model to perform better zero-shot inference.

Weaknesses:
- How does this Dataset reflect knowledge? Since ChatGPT's input is based on factual descriptions of images, how can we ensure the questions generated by ChatGPT are related to external knowledge? On the other hand, how do the authors define what are knowledge-related questions?

- For some knowledge-related questions, it seems that ChatGPT needs to guess the state of the subject in the image. Could this process result in hallucination issues? I notice that there's a critic filtering in the method, which rates the results by training on annotated data. What types of patterns is this model expected to learn, and under what circumstances does it consider the generated data to be inadequate? From Section 2.3, it seems to be judging visual correctness, but how can the correctness of the generated knowledge be determined?

For example, in Fig1, the model assumes that [0] and [2] are teaching [1]. This is a guess made by ChatGPT, as no similar descriptions are generated by the visual models, indicating that they cannot capture this information. So how would the critic model determine if this kind of guess is based on reality or is just a hallucination?

Questions:
My major concern with this work is how to guarantee the dataset quality since there are a lot of automatic generation methods in dataset construction. So I would like to hear more illustrations about that.

title: Django: Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration

reviews.summary: The key idea of this paper is to propose Django, an adaptation of the trigger reverse-engineering technique used for detecting backdoored models in classification to object detection models. The paper shows that existing trigger reverse-engineering techniques are ineffective in object detection and finds that loss misalignment is the primary reason for the less effectiveness. The paper then proposes a new loss function that leverages Focal loss, a well-studied loss function in object detection, against compromised object detection models. In evaluation, Django is more effective than the baseline methods in backdoor detection with less computational overhead. The paper shows Django is effective against a few adaptive attacks.
reviews.strengths:
1. The paper proposes a backdoor detection method for object detection models.
2. The paper presents why existing detection (trigger reverse-engineering) methods are ineffective.
3. Django implements a novel loss function that addresses the problems identified in 2.
4. The paper runs an evaluation against existing techniques and adaptive attacks. 

I like the contribution of this paper proposing a backdoor detection method for object detection models. I don't think this is a groundbreaking contribution, but it is also not a trivial contribution. I believe Django could be a good baseline for backdoor detection in object detection.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. I expect the related work could be a bit more comprehensive in backdoor attacks and detection "in object detection."
2. The evaluation mostly compares the effectiveness against trigger reverse-engineering techniques; comprehensiveness would be nice.

Related work

> Since the paper addresses backdooring in object detection, the related work should discuss the backdoor attacks and defenses in object detection. As backdooring has been studied for a while, there are many backdoor attack papers and proposed defenses. It would also be nice for readers to identify the novelty of this work much more clearly.

Evaluation

> I believe that there are multiple approaches to backdoor defenses (for example, I can think of a simple way like STRIP). Hence, I'd like to see a comprehensive categorization of backdoor defenses (perhaps in the related work section) and a comparison of Django against those missing in the current evaluation. The focus is not to show Django outperforms all the defenses but to show where Django lies in backdoor detection and how effective/ineffective Django is compared to different approaches.
reviews.questions: No question.
reviews.limitations: The paper discusses the limitations in Line 333--342.
