reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new metric to evaluate the quality and diversity of generated images based on
interpretable embeddings. To obtain the interpretable embeddings for selected attributes, the cluster
centers of the encodings of two separate encoders, one for images and the other for the text attributes
are calculated and the interpretable embeddings are the difference between the encodings of each
image and attribute and their respective cluster centers. The interpretable embeddings represent the
direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to it’s cluster center. The CLIPScore
between the interpretable embeddings of an image and an attribute is calculated and is named as
’Directional CLIPScore’, since the interpretable embeddings represent the ’direction’.
The authors have proposed two metrics : 1. SaKLD - to quatify how closely the attribute distri-
bution in generated images matches with that of training images. 2. PaKLD - to quantify correlations
between different attributes. The KL divergence between the probability density functions of the
Directional CLIPScores for all the images in the train data and generated data.
Using SaKLD, the KL divergence between the attribute distribution in training and generated
images is calculated. PaKLD calculates the KL divergence similar to SaKLD, except that the presence
of a pair of attributes is required in the training and generated images.
reviews.strengths: The motivation for the idea is good and has a huge potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although, the motivation for developing this metric is valid, the overall methodology and the
experimental results are not convincing for the use of this metric. Also, the experiments performed
are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to the previously
proposed metrics. Additionally, previous metrics can directly evaluate quality of generation based
on the generated images alone, but this metric heavily relies on the attributes in the form of text
descriptions. Thus, it limits the applicability and generalizability of this metric.
- The proposed metrics use CLIPScore (which already exists) for interpretable embeddings and then
applies KL Divergence for the PDFs of the ClipScores of images and attributes, thus, showing limited
novelty.
- Most of the paper is easy to follow but some important parts like, how is the center of text attributes
calculated, results from table 1 (what does accuracy stand for) etc. are a bit ambiguous. There is a
lot of scope to improve the technical soundness of the paper. Although some of the popular metrics
1are mentioned, there has been a lot of work in the generative modelling domain which the literature
survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the
experimental setup. The results are also not sufficiently explained. Diversity is the main motivation
for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is
completely missing.
- Motivation and methodology including the steps to evaluate generated samples is understandable but
some parts are ambiguous (please refer above comments).
- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there
is not enough validation to support the claims.

 typos:
- Line 43 : Instead of ”Figure 1 (b)”, it should be Figure 1 (a)
- Line 168 : Section 3.3 First letters of all the words in the heading must be capital
Other remarks :
- Line 56 : Instead of ”If the model lacks essential attributes” the following sounds technically
correct ”if model lacks ”representation” of essential attributes”.
- Line 56-58 : This claim does not seem to be correct.
- Line 147 : Link to the specified figure is missing
- Figure 2 is never refered to in the text, is it an unnecessary figure?
- Line 253 : Generated set has non-smiling men and non-smiling women. But Figure 4 caption
says otherwise
reviews.questions:
1. If I understood correctly, the text and the image encoders are different? If yes, then
the embedding space of both models is going to be very different and will be influenced by the
downstream tasks that they are trained on. Would this metric always work for embeddings from
different models? Does it make sense to calculate similarity between embeddings from different
models?
2. Table 1 represents accuracy results for CS vs DCS. What is meant by evaluating ”positive
samples” in line 164 and 165? And what is meant by ”how well they are aligned”? Also, what
exactly is the accuracy calculated for?
3. In Section 3.1, there is a mention of ”each channel of the embedding being utilized”, how exactly
does this happen?
4. Line 196-197 claims that the generated distribution is desirable only if it has exactly same number
of samples as in the training set that has a particular attribute. Why should it be? Afaik, the
underlying distribution of latents represent semantic features of the data and the generative
model is responsible to generate novel samples that could be realistic but not necessarily show
exactly same features same number of times in the training set. So, is the assumption in the
mentioned lines appropriate? Can you prove this or maybe refer to some papers that provide
such guarantees?

Summary:
This paper proposes a new metric to evaluate the quality and diversity of generated images based on
interpretable embeddings. To obtain the interpretable embeddings for selected attributes, the cluster
centers of the encodings of two separate encoders, one for images and the other for the text attributes
are calculated and the interpretable embeddings are the difference between the encodings of each
image and attribute and their respective cluster centers. The interpretable embeddings represent the
direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to it’s cluster center. The CLIPScore
between the interpretable embeddings of an image and an attribute is calculated and is named as
’Directional CLIPScore’, since the interpretable embeddings represent the ’direction’.
The authors have proposed two metrics : 1. SaKLD - to quatify how closely the attribute distri-
bution in generated images matches with that of training images. 2. PaKLD - to quantify correlations
between different attributes. The KL divergence between the probability density functions of the
Directional CLIPScores for all the images in the train data and generated data.
Using SaKLD, the KL divergence between the attribute distribution in training and generated
images is calculated. PaKLD calculates the KL divergence similar to SaKLD, except that the presence
of a pair of attributes is required in the training and generated images.

Strengths:
The motivation for the idea is good and has a huge potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models.

Weaknesses:
- Although, the motivation for developing this metric is valid, the overall methodology and the
experimental results are not convincing for the use of this metric. Also, the experiments performed
are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to the previously
proposed metrics. Additionally, previous metrics can directly evaluate quality of generation based
on the generated images alone, but this metric heavily relies on the attributes in the form of text
descriptions. Thus, it limits the applicability and generalizability of this metric.
- The proposed metrics use CLIPScore (which already exists) for interpretable embeddings and then
applies KL Divergence for the PDFs of the ClipScores of images and attributes, thus, showing limited
novelty.
- Most of the paper is easy to follow but some important parts like, how is the center of text attributes
calculated, results from table 1 (what does accuracy stand for) etc. are a bit ambiguous. There is a
lot of scope to improve the technical soundness of the paper. Although some of the popular metrics
1are mentioned, there has been a lot of work in the generative modelling domain which the literature
survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the
experimental setup. The results are also not sufficiently explained. Diversity is the main motivation
for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is
completely missing.
- Motivation and methodology including the steps to evaluate generated samples is understandable but
some parts are ambiguous (please refer above comments).
- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there
is not enough validation to support the claims.

 typos:
- Line 43 : Instead of ”Figure 1 (b)”, it should be Figure 1 (a)
- Line 168 : Section 3.3 First letters of all the words in the heading must be capital
Other remarks :
- Line 56 : Instead of ”If the model lacks essential attributes” the following sounds technically
correct ”if model lacks ”representation” of essential attributes”.
- Line 56-58 : This claim does not seem to be correct.
- Line 147 : Link to the specified figure is missing
- Figure 2 is never refered to in the text, is it an unnecessary figure?
- Line 253 : Generated set has non-smiling men and non-smiling women. But Figure 4 caption
says otherwise

Questions:
1. If I understood correctly, the text and the image encoders are different? If yes, then
the embedding space of both models is going to be very different and will be influenced by the
downstream tasks that they are trained on. Would this metric always work for embeddings from
different models? Does it make sense to calculate similarity between embeddings from different
models?
2. Table 1 represents accuracy results for CS vs DCS. What is meant by evaluating ”positive
samples” in line 164 and 165? And what is meant by ”how well they are aligned”? Also, what
exactly is the accuracy calculated for?
3. In Section 3.1, there is a mention of ”each channel of the embedding being utilized”, how exactly
does this happen?
4. Line 196-197 claims that the generated distribution is desirable only if it has exactly same number
of samples as in the training set that has a particular attribute. Why should it be? Afaik, the
underlying distribution of latents represent semantic features of the data and the generative
model is responsible to generate novel samples that could be realistic but not necessarily show
exactly same features same number of times in the training set. So, is the assumption in the
mentioned lines appropriate? Can you prove this or maybe refer to some papers that provide
such guarantees?

Limitations:
limitations have been addressed

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

The paper introduces a novel evaluation framework for assessing both quality and diversity in image generation tasks. The approach leverages interpretable embeddings derived from separate image and text encoders. The key innovation involves computing embeddings relative to their respective cluster centers, capturing directional information within the embedding space. The authors then apply CLIPScore to these directional embeddings and propose two metrics: (1) SaKLD, which measures how well the distribution of attributes in generated images aligns with those in training data, and (2) PaKLD, which evaluates correlations between attribute pairs. Both metrics employ KL divergence to compare probability distributions of directional CLIPScores between training and generated datasets.

## Strengths

The underlying motivation is compelling and addresses an important problem in generative model evaluation. The work has significant potential to advance how we assess generative models, particularly regarding diversity metrics.

## Weaknesses

- **Insufficient Experimental Validation**: While the motivation is sound, the methodology and empirical validation fall short of establishing convincing evidence for the metric's utility. The experimental design is inadequate and does not convincingly demonstrate superiority over existing evaluation approaches. A critical limitation is the metric's dependence on text attribute descriptions, which constrains its applicability compared to metrics that operate directly on generated images without such requirements.

- **Limited Technical Novelty**: The approach primarily combines existing components—CLIPScore for embedding generation followed by KL divergence computation on score distributions—without introducing substantial novel contributions to the field.

- **Technical Clarity and Rigor Issues**: Several methodological components require clarification. For instance, the procedure for computing text attribute cluster centers is unclear, and Table 1's "accuracy" metric lacks proper definition. The literature review is incomplete, missing relevant work in generative modeling. The experimental setup lacks sufficient rigor to support the claims. Critically, while diversity is emphasized in the abstract as a key motivation, the paper provides neither theoretical nor empirical evidence substantiating this claim.

- **Incomplete Presentation**: Although the overall narrative is generally comprehensible, ambiguities persist in critical sections. The methodology for evaluating generated samples is understandable at a high level, but important details remain vague.

- **Weak Validation of Claims**: The current experimental evidence is insufficient to justify the paper's contributions. The validation framework does not adequately support the proposed claims.

## Technical Issues and Clarifications Needed

**Typos:**
- Line 43: Reference should read "Figure 1 (a)" rather than "Figure 1 (b)"
- Line 168: Section heading requires proper capitalization of all major words

**Technical Corrections and Concerns:**
- Line 56: The phrasing "if model lacks representation of essential attributes" is more technically precise than the current wording
- Lines 56-58: The stated claim appears problematic and requires revision
- Line 147: A referenced figure link is missing
- Figure 2: This figure is never cited within the text—clarify its necessity or remove it
- Line 253: Inconsistency between the text description and Figure 4 caption regarding the composition of the generated set

## Critical Questions for Authors

1. **Cross-Model Embedding Compatibility**: If the text and image encoders are distinct, their embedding spaces will differ substantially based on their respective training objectives. Does this metric reliably function across different encoder pairs? Is calculating similarity between embeddings from fundamentally different models theoretically justified?

2. **Table 1 Ambiguities**: What precisely does "accuracy" measure in the comparison between CLIPScore and Directional CLIPScore? The terms "positive samples" and "alignment quality" require explicit definition, as does the specific calculation method for accuracy.

3. **Channel Utilization in Section 3.1**: The mention of "each channel of the embedding being utilized" needs clarification regarding the precise mechanism of this process.

4. **Distribution Matching Assumption (Lines 196-197)**: The claim that generated distributions are desirable only when they match the exact frequency of training set attributes seems overly restrictive. Generative models are expected to learn underlying semantic distributions while producing novel samples that may not perfectly replicate training set frequencies. Is this assumption justified theoretically or empirically? Can relevant literature support this premise?

## Limitations

The authors have adequately addressed limitations within the manuscript.

## Recommendation

**4: Borderline reject** - This paper presents a technically competent approach to an important problem; however, the limitations in experimental validation, restricted applicability due to text dependency, modest technical novelty, and insufficient evidence supporting core claims collectively outweigh the strengths. Substantial revisions to the experimental framework, clearer technical exposition, and more comprehensive validation would be necessary for acceptance.

title: Residual Scheduling: A New Reinforcement Learning Approach to Solving Job Shop Scheduling Problem

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning-based constructive heuristic to solve the (Flexible) Job Shop Scheduling Problem. An instance of the problem is represented as a graph and fed into a Graph Neural Network-based model which outputs a score for each candidate (operation-machine) pair. The model is trained with the REINFORCE algorithm with as baseline a classic Priority Dispatching Rule-based heuristic. The novelty of the paper lies in the update of the state after each action: the graph is updated by removing the operations which have already been executed to focus on the most relevant information, which is the residual operations and remaining times of the ongoing ones. The proposed approach is experimentally shown to outperform RL-based constructive heuristics on classic JSSP and FJSSP benchmarks.
reviews.strengths:
1. Sound and interesting idea of removing irrelevant information from the state 
1. The paper is fairly clear and I appreciated the illustrations Fig 1-3.
1. The model was proposed for the JSSP and easily adapted to the Flexible JSSP
1. The approach outperforms deep RL-based construction heuristics on classic benchmarks
reviews.weaknesses:
1. Limited novelty: the main contribution is the definition of the residual state at each step of the construction process by removing irrelevant operations and resetting the time reference. This seems to me an incremental improvement of the approach L2D [1], which already proposed a similar state graph representation and the use of the Graph Isomorphism Network architecture for the JSSP.
1. The proposed approach seems very specific to (F)JSSPs (state representation, baseline) and it's not clear what could be transferable to DRL heuristics for solving other optimization problems.
1. In the experiments, the presented non-learning-based baselines seem pretty weak: only greedy (see more in Questions)
1. Lots of English typos (I noted a few per page)

[1] C Zhang et al, Learning to Dispatch for Job Shop Scheduling via Deep Reinforcement Learning, Neurips 2020
reviews.questions:
1. Where dos the attributes of edges (L205) appear in the GNN model (Sec 3.3)?
1. The paper claims: “Interestingly, using RS, the average gaps are nearly zero for the collections with sizes larger than 100 … A strong implication is that our RS approach can be scaled up for job sizes and even reach the optimal for sufficient large job count.” Do you have an idea of why the model would work better on unseen large instances versus instances of the same size as the training ones? Does OR-tools return the optimal solutions for these larger instances? Can it be that the quality of the reference solutions decreases and therefore the “optimality” gap becomes smaller?
1. Are there stronger non-learning-based baselines for the JSPP other than the greedy PDR heuristics presented in Table 2? To be able to appreciate the performance of the proposed approach, it would be useful and more convincing to compare it to the best heuristics for this problem, beyond simple greedy ones. For example maybe [2]?
1. Appendix, Algorithm 1, Line 6:  to compute this makespan at state s_t, given action a_t, do you do a rollout of the current policy \pi_{\theta} with the updated parameter \theta? This would mean doing a rollout until the end of both the baseline and current policy at each step of the trajectory? How long did the training take?
1. How is the average computation time computed? (Table 13) In particular, was the policy applied to each instance individually or were instances batched? 
1. The discussion L322 about the time it takes for RS/L2D/ScheduleNet versus OR-Tools can be a bit misleading: OR-tools is an exact solver. If spends a lot of time proving the optimality of the solution. Probably it could return a good quality solution much faster if used as a heuristic. In addition it runs on CPUs and not GPUs therefore just comparing the time is not really fair. 

[2] CY Zhang, A tabu search algorithm with a new neighborhood structure for the job shop scheduling problem, Computers & Operations Research, 2007
reviews.limitations: Not explicitly addressed by the paper.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

1. reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

The authors introduce a deep reinforcement learning approach for tackling the Job Shop Scheduling Problem and its Flexible variant. The core methodology involves representing problem instances as graphs that feed into a Graph Neural Network architecture, which generates scores for potential (operation-machine) assignments. Training utilizes the REINFORCE algorithm, employing a Priority Dispatching Rule-based heuristic as the baseline. The key innovation centers on dynamically updating the state representation by pruning already-completed operations, thereby directing the model's attention toward unexecuted operations and current time windows. Experimental validation demonstrates improvements over existing deep RL-based constructive approaches on standard JSSP and FJSSP test sets.

## Strengths

1. The concept of progressively eliminating outdated information from the state representation is well-motivated and intuitive
2. The manuscript is generally well-written with effective visual aids in Figures 1-3 that enhance understanding
3. The framework demonstrates flexibility, being originally developed for JSSP and subsequently extended to the Flexible variant without significant modifications
4. Empirical results show measurable performance gains relative to competing deep RL-based construction methods on established benchmarks

## Weaknesses

1. The novelty appears somewhat limited. The central contribution—constructing a residual state by filtering completed operations and adjusting temporal references—represents an incremental refinement of prior work, particularly the L2D approach [1], which already introduced comparable graph-based state representations and employed Graph Isomorphism Networks for JSSP applications.

2. The methodology exhibits considerable problem-specificity (particularly in state design and baseline selection), raising questions about generalizability to other combinatorial optimization domains and the potential transferability of insights to broader DRL heuristic development.

3. The experimental comparison relies on relatively simplistic baselines: the non-learning alternatives presented consist primarily of greedy dispatching rules, which may not provide sufficiently rigorous benchmarking (see additional concerns below).

4. The manuscript contains numerous grammatical errors and typos distributed throughout, appearing roughly several times per page.

## Questions and Concerns

1. The paper references edge attributes in Line 205 but their incorporation into the GNN architecture (Section 3.3) remains unclear. Could the authors clarify how these attributes are utilized in the model?

2. The claim that "using RS, the average gaps are nearly zero for collections with sizes larger than 100" warrants deeper investigation. What explains the superior performance on larger unseen instances relative to training-sized instances? Additionally, does OR-tools actually identify optimal solutions for these larger problems, or could the observed gap reduction simply reflect degraded solution quality in the reference solutions themselves?

3. Table 2 presents only greedy Priority Dispatching Rules as non-learning baselines. Stronger heuristic alternatives exist in the literature for JSSP—for instance, metaheuristic approaches such as tabu search [2]—which would provide more compelling evidence of the proposed method's practical utility and competitiveness.

4. Algorithm 1, Line 6 requires clarification: when computing the makespan at state s_t following action a_t, does the procedure execute a complete rollout of the current policy π_θ using updated parameters? If so, this would necessitate rolling out both the baseline and current policy to completion at each trajectory step. What was the total computational cost of training under this scheme?

5. The methodology for calculating average computation time in Table 13 needs specification. Were policy evaluations performed on individual instances or using batch processing across multiple instances?

6. The timing comparison in Line 322 between RS/L2D/ScheduleNet and OR-Tools requires contextualization. OR-Tools functions as an exact solver, dedicating substantial time to optimality verification; it could likely produce high-quality solutions considerably faster if operated as a heuristic rather than an exact method. Furthermore, OR-Tools executes on CPUs while the proposed approach utilizes GPUs, making direct temporal comparisons problematic from a fairness perspective.

## Limitations

The paper does not explicitly discuss its limitations.

## Overall Assessment

This work presents a technically sound contribution with competent experimental execution. The core insight regarding state reduction through operation filtering is sensible and yields measurable improvements. However, the incremental nature of the advance relative to existing graph-based RL methods, combined with limited exploration of generalizability beyond scheduling problems and reliance on weak experimental baselines, tempers the significance of the contribution. The paper would benefit from more comprehensive baseline comparisons and clearer exposition of certain technical details. Notwithstanding these concerns, the work demonstrates sufficient merit for publication.

## Recommendation

**6: Weak Accept** — The paper is technically competent with moderate-to-high potential impact, and raises no significant concerns regarding evaluation rigor, resource utilization, reproducibility, or ethical dimensions.

reviews.summary: This paper proposed DRL based method to learn dispatching polices for (flexible) job-shop scheduling problems (JSP/FJSP). The main idea is to remove the completed operations from the state embedding, which is called residual scheduling, so as to improve the representation accuracy. The DRL agent uses a graph representation, which is processed by a Graph Neural Network (GNN) architecture. Experiments on JSP and FJSP benchmarks show that the proposed residual scheduling scheme outperforms recent DRL baselines.
reviews.strengths:
1. The idea of residual scheduling makes great sense and is interesting.

2. The method is generally applicable to both JSP and FJSP, which are important scheduling problems.

3. Good empirical performance, comparing to recent DRL based scheduling methods.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The main weakness is that the technical contribution is incremental. While the redisual scheduling idea is interesting and novel, a large part of the proposed method is similar to existing works. Specifically, the graph representation and heterogeneous graph neural network in Section 3.2 and 3.3 is similar to the heterogeneous graph and heterogeneous GNN in (Song et al., 2023). This is not mentioned in Section 3, and the differences between the proposed method and existing works are not discussed.

2. Some design choices need to be justified. Please see the below questions.

3. Empirical evaluation needs to be improved. 

* It is unclear whether the models in other works (e.g. L2D, SchN, DRL-G) are retrained using the same dataset as the proposed method. If not, then directly comparing their performance (even on the same benchmark instances) is not fair due to different training data.

* The discussion for Figure 4 is not surprising. It is well known that for JSP, problems with larger $n/m$ ratios are easier to solve (Taillard 1993). That is why the gaps on large problems in Figure 4 are smaller. Actually this is true for most algorithms, and cannot be claimed as a major advantage of the proposed method.

* Training time is not reported.

4. The authors made several inappropriate statements in the paper, mainly in introduction. The authors should be more precise about the related concepts.

* In the first paragraph, it is better to describe JSP as a combinatorial optimization problem, instead of mathematical optimization.

* In the second paragraph, Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) should be not stated as a type of mathematical optimization. In addition, Constraint Programming (CP) here is more suitable than CSP. 

* In the fourth paragraph, the approaches that automate the design of heuristics should be hyperheuristics, instead of metaheuristics.

4. The language needs to be improved. Besides, there are quite a few grammar errors and typos.
reviews.questions:
1. Why use fully connected edges to link the operations in the same job? How about using only precedence relationship as a directed edge?

2. The second graph below Figure 3, for flexible problems, how to define the ready status for an operation considering there could be multiple compatible machines?

3. According to Figure 3(b), the graph embedding $h_{\mathcal{G}}^{(k)}$ is not used in the action score prediction as in Figure 3(c). So what is $h_{\mathcal{G}}^{(k)}$ used for?

4. In the experiments, to generate number of jobs and machines, why use the uniform distribution $\mathcal{U}(3, N)$ and $\mathcal{U}(3,n)$ (should be $\mathcal{U}(3,M)$ for machines)? The value 3 seems arbitrary.
reviews.limitations: The authors did not give a particular discussion on the limitations. One limitation could be the computational efficiency. As shown in Table 13 in the appendix, the runtime increase rapidly with the number of jobs. This could affect both training and inference efficiency, and limit its applicability to larger problems.

Summary:
This paper proposed DRL based method to learn dispatching polices for (flexible) job-shop scheduling problems (JSP/FJSP). The main idea is to remove the completed operations from the state embedding, which is called residual scheduling, so as to improve the representation accuracy. The DRL agent uses a graph representation, which is processed by a Graph Neural Network (GNN) architecture. Experiments on JSP and FJSP benchmarks show that the proposed residual scheduling scheme outperforms recent DRL baselines.

Strengths:
1. The idea of residual scheduling makes great sense and is interesting.

2. The method is generally applicable to both JSP and FJSP, which are important scheduling problems.

3. Good empirical performance, comparing to recent DRL based scheduling methods.

Weaknesses:
1. The main weakness is that the technical contribution is incremental. While the redisual scheduling idea is interesting and novel, a large part of the proposed method is similar to existing works. Specifically, the graph representation and heterogeneous graph neural network in Section 3.2 and 3.3 is similar to the heterogeneous graph and heterogeneous GNN in (Song et al., 2023). This is not mentioned in Section 3, and the differences between the proposed method and existing works are not discussed.

2. Some design choices need to be justified. Please see the below questions.

3. Empirical evaluation needs to be improved. 

* It is unclear whether the models in other works (e.g. L2D, SchN, DRL-G) are retrained using the same dataset as the proposed method. If not, then directly comparing their performance (even on the same benchmark instances) is not fair due to different training data.

* The discussion for Figure 4 is not surprising. It is well known that for JSP, problems with larger $n/m$ ratios are easier to solve (Taillard 1993). That is why the gaps on large problems in Figure 4 are smaller. Actually this is true for most algorithms, and cannot be claimed as a major advantage of the proposed method.

* Training time is not reported.

4. The authors made several inappropriate statements in the paper, mainly in introduction. The authors should be more precise about the related concepts.

* In the first paragraph, it is better to describe JSP as a combinatorial optimization problem, instead of mathematical optimization.

* In the second paragraph, Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) should be not stated as a type of mathematical optimization. In addition, Constraint Programming (CP) here is more suitable than CSP. 

* In the fourth paragraph, the approaches that automate the design of heuristics should be hyperheuristics, instead of metaheuristics.

4. The language needs to be improved. Besides, there are quite a few grammar errors and typos.

Questions:
1. Why use fully connected edges to link the operations in the same job? How about using only precedence relationship as a directed edge?

2. The second graph below Figure 3, for flexible problems, how to define the ready status for an operation considering there could be multiple compatible machines?

3. According to Figure 3(b), the graph embedding $h_{\mathcal{G}}^{(k)}$ is not used in the action score prediction as in Figure 3(c). So what is $h_{\mathcal{G}}^{(k)}$ used for?

4. In the experiments, to generate number of jobs and machines, why use the uniform distribution $\mathcal{U}(3, N)$ and $\mathcal{U}(3,n)$ (should be $\mathcal{U}(3,M)$ for machines)? The value 3 seems arbitrary.

Limitations:
The authors did not give a particular discussion on the limitations. One limitation could be the computational efficiency. As shown in Table 13 in the appendix, the runtime increase rapidly with the number of jobs. This could affect both training and inference efficiency, and limit its applicability to larger problems.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

The paper introduces a DRL-based approach for learning dispatching strategies in job-shop scheduling (JSP) and flexible job-shop scheduling (FJSP) problems. The core innovation centers on residual scheduling—the exclusion of already-completed operations from state representations to enhance representation fidelity. The method leverages a graph-based formulation processed through Graph Neural Networks. Experimental validation on standard JSP and FJSP benchmarks demonstrates that the residual scheduling approach achieves superior results compared to recent DRL-based alternatives.

## Strengths

1. The residual scheduling concept is compelling and represents a meaningful contribution to the field.

2. The approach demonstrates broad applicability across both JSP and FJSP, which are practically significant problem classes.

3. The experimental results are competitive, showing improvements over contemporary DRL-based scheduling approaches.

## Weaknesses

1. **Limited Technical Novelty**: The primary concern is that the technical contribution remains largely incremental. Although the residual scheduling idea is novel and well-motivated, substantial portions of the methodology closely parallel existing approaches. In particular, the graph representation formulation and the heterogeneous graph neural network architecture presented in Sections 3.2 and 3.3 exhibit considerable similarity to the heterogeneous graph and heterogeneous GNN structures introduced in Song et al. (2023). Notably, this relationship is not acknowledged in Section 3, and a clear delineation of distinctions from prior work is absent.

2. **Insufficient Justification of Design Decisions**: Several architectural and methodological choices warrant more rigorous justification and explanation.

3. **Gaps in Experimental Rigor**:

   * A critical ambiguity concerns whether baseline methods (L2D, SchN, DRL-G, etc.) were retrained on identical datasets as the proposed approach. Without this standardization, performance comparisons—even on identical benchmark instances—lack validity due to potential training data divergence.

   * The analysis in Figure 4 lacks novelty and insight. The observation that larger n/m ratios correlate with problem tractability in JSP is well-established in the literature (Taillard 1993) and is broadly characteristic of scheduling algorithms generally. Consequently, the smaller performance gaps on larger instances cannot be meaningfully attributed as a distinctive strength of the proposed method.

   * Computational runtime comparisons are entirely absent from the evaluation.

4. **Imprecise and Inaccurate Terminology**: The paper contains several problematic statements, concentrated primarily in the introduction, that reflect conceptual imprecision:

   * JSP should be characterized as a combinatorial optimization problem rather than a mathematical optimization problem.

   * Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSP) are incorrectly presented as a category of mathematical optimization. Furthermore, Constraint Programming (CP) would be the more appropriate terminology than CSP in this context.

   * Methods that automate heuristic design should be classified as hyperheuristics, not metaheuristics.

5. **Language and Presentation Quality**: The writing quality requires substantial improvement, with numerous grammatical errors and typographical mistakes throughout.

## Specific Questions for Authors

1. What is the rationale for employing fully connected edges among operations within the same job? Have you considered restricting connectivity to only direct precedence relationships as directed edges?

2. In the context of flexible scheduling problems, how is the "ready" status of an operation determined when multiple compatible machines exist?

3. Based on Figure 3(b), the global graph embedding h_G^(k) does not appear in the action scoring computation shown in Figure 3(c). What function does h_G^(k) serve in the overall framework?

4. Why were uniform distributions U(3,N) and U(3,n) (should be U(3,M) for machines) selected for generating problem parameters? The choice of 3 as a lower bound appears arbitrary and lacks justification.

## Limitations

The authors provide minimal discussion of the work's limitations. A significant concern involves computational scalability: Table 13 in the appendix reveals that runtime grows substantially with problem size. This scaling behavior has implications for both training and inference efficiency, potentially restricting the method's practical application to larger problem instances.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a technically sound contribution with a novel residual scheduling concept that demonstrates empirical promise. However, the incremental nature of the broader technical contribution, combined with incomplete experimental analysis and presentation issues, prevents this from being a strong accept. The core idea has merit, and the experimental results are encouraging, but the evaluation could be more comprehensive, and the relationship to prior work requires clearer articulation. With revisions addressing these concerns, this could become a solid contribution to the field.

## Recommendation

**5: Borderline accept** - This is a technically competent paper where the merits of acceptance modestly outweigh the limitations, though the evaluation could be more thorough and the technical novelty is somewhat constrained.

reviews.summary: This paper studied the problem of learning a graph neural network based policy network as a construction heuristic for solving job shop scheduling problems. This paper proposed an idea called residual scheduling to remove irrelevant operations and machines from the graph based state representation. This has been shown experimentally to perform well on several benchmark job shop scheduling problem instances.
reviews.strengths: With the fast advancement of deep learning technologies, it becomes increasingly interesting to develop deep neural network models that can effectively solve complex combinatorial optimization problems, including job shop scheduling problems. The newly developed deep learning system in this paper appears to be very effective and highly competitive in performance, compared to similar approaches from the literature.
reviews.weaknesses:
The idea of using graph neural networks or other forms of deep neural networks trained through reinforcement learning to solve job shop scheduling problems has been studied in many past research works. The main text of this paper lacks a comprehensive review of these research works, making it hard to clearly understand the key technical novelty and contribution of this paper, compared to other recently published works.

The development of the residual scheduling technique is not strongly motivated in this paper. It is hard to understand why it is essential to remove irrelevant operations and machines from the graph based state representation. The development of this technique also appears to be very highly intuitive and lacks thorough theoretical analysis. Hence, the technical contribution of this development remains largely questionable.

The authors stated that in order for the process of learning the construction heuristic to be formulated as an MDP, they introduced several attributes for the operation nodes and machine nodes in the graph based state representation. However, it remains largely unknown whether, with the introduced attributes, the state representation can satisfy the Markov property of the MDP. The importance of using any newly introduced attributes should be more thoroughly evaluated experimentally. The associated technical contributions should be clarified and strongly justified.

Some other key aspects of the new system design should also be justified more. For example, the use of GIN needs to be supported with more convincing reasons. The focus on learning a construction heuristic rather than an improvement heuristic, which is gaining increasing popularity and attention, should be better justified and experimentally validated in this paper.
reviews.questions:
What are the key novelties of this research work, compared to existing deep learning based methods for combinatorial optimization, including job shop scheduling?

At the theoretical level, why is it essential to remove irrelevant operations and machines from the graph based state representation?

With the newly introduced attributes, why will the state representation satisfy the Markov property of the MDP?

What are the theoretical and empirical advantages of learning a construction heuristic, in comparison to learning an improvement heuristic?
reviews.limitations: I do not have any concerns regarding this question.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studied the problem of learning a graph neural network based policy network as a construction heuristic for solving job shop scheduling problems. This paper proposed an idea called residual scheduling to remove irrelevant operations and machines from the graph based state representation. This has been shown experimentally to perform well on several benchmark job shop scheduling problem instances.

Strengths:
With the fast advancement of deep learning technologies, it becomes increasingly interesting to develop deep neural network models that can effectively solve complex combinatorial optimization problems, including job shop scheduling problems. The newly developed deep learning system in this paper appears to be very effective and highly competitive in performance, compared to similar approaches from the literature.

Weaknesses:
The idea of using graph neural networks or other forms of deep neural networks trained through reinforcement learning to solve job shop scheduling problems has been studied in many past research works. The main text of this paper lacks a comprehensive review of these research works, making it hard to clearly understand the key technical novelty and contribution of this paper, compared to other recently published works.

The development of the residual scheduling technique is not strongly motivated in this paper. It is hard to understand why it is essential to remove irrelevant operations and machines from the graph based state representation. The development of this technique also appears to be very highly intuitive and lacks thorough theoretical analysis. Hence, the technical contribution of this development remains largely questionable.

The authors stated that in order for the process of learning the construction heuristic to be formulated as an MDP, they introduced several attributes for the operation nodes and machine nodes in the graph based state representation. However, it remains largely unknown whether, with the introduced attributes, the state representation can satisfy the Markov property of the MDP. The importance of using any newly introduced attributes should be more thoroughly evaluated experimentally. The associated technical contributions should be clarified and strongly justified.

Some other key aspects of the new system design should also be justified more. For example, the use of GIN needs to be supported with more convincing reasons. The focus on learning a construction heuristic rather than an improvement heuristic, which is gaining increasing popularity and attention, should be better justified and experimentally validated in this paper.

Questions:
What are the key novelties of this research work, compared to existing deep learning based methods for combinatorial optimization, including job shop scheduling?

At the theoretical level, why is it essential to remove irrelevant operations and machines from the graph based state representation?

With the newly introduced attributes, why will the state representation satisfy the Markov property of the MDP?

What are the theoretical and empirical advantages of learning a construction heuristic, in comparison to learning an improvement heuristic?

Limitations:
I do not have any concerns regarding this question.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Summary:
This work investigates the application of graph neural networks to construct a policy for solving job shop scheduling problems using a construction heuristic approach. The authors introduce a technique termed residual scheduling, which filters out extraneous operations and machines from the graph-based representation of the problem state. The proposed method demonstrates competitive empirical results on established job shop scheduling benchmarks.

Strengths:
The intersection of deep learning and combinatorial optimization represents a compelling research direction, particularly as neural network architectures continue to evolve. The proposed deep learning framework exhibits strong empirical performance and appears to be competitive with existing methods in the literature, suggesting practical viability of the approach.

Weaknesses:
While the application of graph neural networks and deep reinforcement learning to job shop scheduling has been extensively explored in prior literature, the current paper provides insufficient coverage of these existing works. This limited contextual discussion makes it challenging to identify precisely what distinguishes this work technically and substantively from recent contributions in the field.

The residual scheduling mechanism lacks adequate motivation and justification. Specifically, the paper does not convincingly explain why excluding certain operations and machines from the graph representation is necessary or beneficial. The technique appears to emerge primarily from intuitive reasoning rather than principled analysis, leaving its technical merit unclear.

A significant concern pertains to the MDP formulation. The authors introduce various node attributes to enable the construction heuristic learning process to be cast as an MDP, yet the paper fails to establish whether these attributes ensure the resulting state representation satisfies the Markov property—a fundamental requirement for valid MDP formulations. The necessity and impact of each newly introduced attribute warrant more rigorous experimental validation and clearer technical exposition.

Additional design choices require stronger justification. For instance, the selection of Graph Isomorphism Networks warrants more compelling argumentation. Furthermore, the decision to focus on construction heuristics rather than improvement heuristics—an increasingly prominent research direction—needs both theoretical grounding and empirical support to validate this choice.

Questions:
What specific innovations distinguish this work from existing deep learning approaches to combinatorial optimization and job shop scheduling in particular?

What theoretical or empirical justification supports the necessity of removing irrelevant operations and machines from the state graph?

How do the newly introduced node attributes guarantee that the state representation maintains the Markov property required for MDP validity?

What are the concrete theoretical and empirical benefits of pursuing a construction heuristic over an improvement heuristic strategy?

Limitations:
No significant concerns regarding this aspect.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically sound contribution where acceptance rationale marginally outweighs rejection rationale, such as due to incomplete experimental scope. Should be used judiciously.

**Decision: Accept (with reservations)**

Summary:
The paper introduces a novel approach called residual scheduling for solving the Job-shop scheduling problem (JSP) and its variant, flexible JSP (FJSP), focusing on removing irrelevant machines and jobs from the consideration set. Despite these problems being NP-hard, the proposed method demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across standard benchmarks, even performing well when scaled to larger problem sizes, achieving a zero gap in 49 out of 50 instances with more than 150 jobs on 20 machines.

Strengths:
The method developed claims that for the 98% of the cases, the zero gap is achieved for fairly large instances.

Weaknesses:
The zero gap is mentioned. Upon reading, readers find out that the gap refers to "makespan gap." Understandably, significant bulk of existing papers on job-shop focus on makespan rather than tardiness. Ignoring tardiness may lead to poorer on time delivery, which is a weakness in itself, but with respect to the "makespan gap" measure it is not clear whether zero gap will result in zero (or small gap) is tardiness is considered or, generally, if the standard duality or MIP gaps are used instead. The gap is misleading at best.

Questions:
The paper claims to beath SOTA heuristics. Lagrangian heuristic is definitely missing. How does the new method compare against Lagrangian heuristic? Understandably, Lagrangian relaxation has been used for a long time, the reviewer is asking about recent rather than historical Lagrangian heuristics, since their quality may differ.

Limitations:
In light of the above comments, the limitations are 1. only makespan is considered, 2. only one type of gap seems to be introduced and considered, and 3. major heuristics are not used/compared with.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This manuscript proposes a novel residual scheduling methodology designed to address both the classical Job-shop scheduling problem (JSP) and its generalized variant, the flexible JSP (FJSP). The core innovation centers on systematically filtering out irrelevant machines and jobs from the solution space. While these problems are known to be computationally intractable (NP-hard), the authors report competitive results against established benchmarks, with particularly noteworthy performance on larger instances, achieving zero gap in 49 of 50 test cases involving 150+ jobs distributed across 20 machines.

## Strengths

The proposed approach demonstrates impressive empirical results, successfully achieving zero gap performance in approximately 98% of tested cases across fairly substantial problem instances.

## Weaknesses

A critical ambiguity undermines the paper's claims: the reported "zero gap" specifically refers to makespan gap rather than other relevant performance metrics. While it is true that existing job-shop scheduling literature predominantly emphasizes makespan optimization, the exclusive focus on this metric obscures important practical considerations. Specifically, the paper does not adequately address whether achieving zero makespan gap would translate to comparable performance when tardiness is incorporated as an objective, nor does it clarify how the results would change under alternative gap definitions such as standard duality gaps or MIP optimality gaps. This terminology creates potential confusion and may misrepresent the true scope of the method's capabilities.

## Questions and Concerns

The authors claim their method surpasses existing state-of-the-art heuristics, yet a significant methodological gap exists: no comparison is provided against Lagrangian heuristic approaches. While Lagrangian relaxation-based methods have a lengthy history in scheduling literature, contemporary variants may exhibit substantially different performance characteristics. The manuscript would benefit from benchmarking against recent Lagrangian heuristic implementations to substantiate the superiority claims.

## Identified Limitations

Three principal limitations emerge from the preceding observations: (1) the evaluation is restricted exclusively to makespan optimization, neglecting other important objectives such as tardiness; (2) the paper introduces and evaluates only a single gap metric, limiting the comprehensiveness of performance assessment; and (3) several established heuristic approaches, particularly modern Lagrangian methods, are absent from the comparative analysis.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 5 - Borderline Accept**

This is a technically competent contribution where the merits of acceptance marginally outweigh the reasons for rejection, though the evaluation scope remains somewhat constrained. The work demonstrates solid technical execution and achieves respectable empirical performance; however, the limitations noted above—particularly the narrow focus on makespan and incomplete comparison against all relevant baselines—prevent this from being a strong acceptance. The paper would benefit from revision to address these concerns, though the core contribution is sufficient to warrant publication.

The gap is misleading at best.

Questions:
The paper claims to beath SOTA heuristics. Lagrangian heuristic is definitely missing. How does the new method compare against Lagrangian heuristic? Understandably, Lagrangian relaxation has been used for a long time, the reviewer is asking about recent rather than historical Lagrangian heuristics, since their quality may differ.

Limitations:
In light of the above comments, the limitations are 1. only makespan is considered, 2. only one type of gap seems to be introduced and considered, and 3. major heuristics are not used/compared with.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

Summary:
Offline reinforcement learning (RL) methods frequently involve a policy constraint to mitigate error propagation when learning the Q function. Generally, a single constraint strength is used throughout training. This paper proposes instead to use different constraint strengths for learning the target policy, which is only used for learning the Q function, and the evaluation policy, which is the final policy returned by the algorithm. In particular, a stronger constraint is needed to ensure stability when training the target policy, but weakening the constraint for the evaluation policy can lead to better performance.

Strengths:
* The idea is fairly general and can be instantiated with various RL algorithms, as shown in the paper.
* The experimental results provide insight into the role of the constraint and the tradeoff between stability and performance.
* Conceptually, the approach allows for a continuum of algorithms between one-step RL and standard actor-critic methods.
* The paper is clearly written and understandable.

Weaknesses:
* The algorithm introduces an additional hyperparameter that requires tuning, which is already a challenge in offline RL.
* The paper found that “in 6 out of the 9 tasks, the $\alpha$ for better inference performance is higher than the $\alpha$ that enables safe Q estimates”. While 6/9 is a majority, this is not convincing evidence that weakening the constraints is always helpful.

Questions:
In Section 5.2, how exactly do you determine "the lowest $\alpha$ value that causes Q value explosion"? (In particular, how is "explosion" defined?) Could this lead to a hyperparameter tuning procedure that involves only looking at the Q values and does not require off-policy evaluation or sample collection?

Limitations:
Yes, limitations are addressed.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This paper addresses a key challenge in offline reinforcement learning by proposing a differentiated constraint strategy. Rather than applying uniform policy constraint strength across training, the authors suggest using distinct constraint magnitudes for two purposes: a stricter constraint governing the target policy (employed solely for Q-function updates) and a more relaxed constraint for the evaluation policy (the final policy deployed by the algorithm). The motivation is sound—tighter constraints stabilize Q-function learning, while relaxing constraints on the final policy can improve practical performance.

## Strengths

* The proposed approach demonstrates broad applicability and can be integrated with multiple offline RL algorithms, as the paper illustrates through various instantiations.
* The experimental evaluation yields valuable insights into how constraint strength influences the stability-performance tradeoff and the function of constraints in the framework.
* From a theoretical perspective, the method enables a spectrum of algorithm variants that interpolate between one-step RL approaches and conventional actor-critic algorithms.
* The presentation is lucid and accessible, making the core contributions easy to follow.

## Weaknesses

* The method introduces an additional hyperparameter requiring tuning, which exacerbates an already significant challenge in offline RL where hyperparameter sensitivity is problematic.
* The empirical support for the core premise is somewhat limited. The paper reports that "in 6 out of the 9 tasks, the α for better inference performance is higher than the α that enables safe Q estimates." While this represents a majority, it falls short of providing compelling evidence that relaxing constraints uniformly improves outcomes across diverse settings.

## Questions for Authors

In Section 5.2, what is the precise procedure for identifying "the lowest α value that causes Q value explosion"? Specifically, how is "explosion" operationalized and measured? Is it possible to develop a hyperparameter selection procedure based solely on Q-value monitoring that avoids the need for off-policy evaluation or additional data collection?

## Limitations

The authors appropriately discuss and acknowledge the limitations of their work.

## Recommendation

**6: Weak Accept** — The paper presents technically sound work with moderate-to-high potential impact. The evaluation is rigorous, reproducibility concerns are minimal, and there are no significant issues regarding ethical considerations or resource usage.

A separate target policy is used solely for evaluation and stabilizing value estimation, which is more constrained than the "evaluation policy." The evaluation policy does not participate in policy evaluation and is improved by the value function estimates, with the level of constraint adjusted by the weight of the term.

The empirical findings demonstrate promising improvements, with baselines encompassing the standard suite of state-of-the-art offline RL algorithms.
- The paper is well-written, easy to comprehend, and thoughtfully structured. 
- The idea itself is intuitive, and the toy experiments convincingly demonstrate that over-constraint poses a significant issue. Figure 2 clearly illustrates the adverse effects of over-constraint, with the policy performing poorly in low state value regions of the maze.

I believe this simple yet intuitive method is worth presenting to the broader offline RL community. I believe this work should be accepted.

This work addresses the issue of excessive policy constraints in stabilizing value estimation within the offline RL paradigm. A separate target policy is used solely for evaluation and stabilizing value estimation, which is more constrained than the "evaluation policy." The evaluation policy does not participate in policy evaluation and is improved by the value function estimates, with the level of constraint adjusted by the weight of the term.

Strengths:
Major points:
- This procedure can be easily integrated into offline RL algorithms that utilize policy constraints, and empirical results apply it to TD3+BC and AWAC. The empirical findings demonstrate promising improvements, with baselines encompassing the standard suite of state-of-the-art offline RL algorithms.
- The paper is well-written, easy to comprehend, and thoughtfully structured. 
- The idea itself is intuitive, and the toy experiments convincingly demonstrate that over-constraint poses a significant issue. Figure 2 clearly illustrates the adverse effects of over-constraint, with the policy performing poorly in low state value regions of the maze.
- The ablation studies are extensive and demonstrate the method's effectiveness.

I believe this simple yet intuitive method is worth presenting to the broader offline RL community. I believe this work should be accepted.

Weaknesses:
Major points:
- While the results show promise, they do not indicate substantial improvements across many environments, and there is some inconsistency observed. The method shows a decrease in performance in the medium-expert D4RL tasks and the pen task.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This paper tackles the problem of overly restrictive policy constraints that impede effective value function stabilization in the offline reinforcement learning setting. The authors introduce a dual-policy framework where a distinct target policy serves the purposes of value estimation stabilization and evaluation, operating under stricter constraints than the "evaluation policy." The evaluation policy itself is not used for evaluation purposes but rather undergoes improvement driven by the learned value estimates, with the degree of constraint controlled through a tunable weighting parameter.

## Strengths

**Major Contributions:**

- The proposed approach demonstrates significant practical utility by being readily applicable to existing offline RL methods that incorporate policy constraints. The empirical validation successfully demonstrates this versatility through applications to TD3+BC and AWAC, with results showing meaningful gains relative to a comprehensive set of state-of-the-art offline RL baselines.

- The manuscript is clearly articulated and well-organized, making it accessible to readers and easy to follow the logical flow of ideas.

- The core intuition underlying the method is straightforward and compelling. The preliminary experiments effectively illustrate that excessive constraint imposition represents a genuine and consequential problem. In particular, Figure 2 provides convincing visual evidence of performance degradation when constraints are applied too aggressively, as evidenced by poor policy behavior in regions where state values are low within the maze environment.

- The paper includes thorough ablation analyses that convincingly establish the contribution and robustness of the proposed method.

In my view, this straightforward yet elegant approach merits dissemination within the offline RL research community. The work demonstrates sufficient merit for publication.

## Weaknesses

**Major Concerns:**

- The empirical improvements, while encouraging, are not uniformly substantial across the tested environments, and notable variability in performance is evident. Specifically, the method exhibits performance degradation on the medium-expert D4RL benchmark tasks and particularly on the pen manipulation task.

## Questions for Authors

Could the authors provide clarification regarding the performance inconsistencies observed in the results, particularly the degradation observed in the pen task? What underlying factors might explain these performance variations across different task domains?

## Limitations

The authors have adequately addressed limitations in their work.

## Overall Assessment and Recommendation

**Rating: 6 – Weak Accept**

This paper presents a technically sound contribution with moderate-to-high potential impact. The work demonstrates no significant issues regarding experimental design, computational resources, reproducibility, or ethical considerations. The method is intuitive and practically useful, with comprehensive empirical validation and thorough ablation studies. While the performance improvements are not uniformly dramatic and some inconsistencies warrant further investigation, the overall contribution is sufficient to merit acceptance to the community.

Summary:
The paper investigates the problem of policy constraints in offline reinforcement learning (RL) settings, and finds the phenomenon that milder constraints on policies during training can lead to better performances at inference tests. The proposed component MCEP can be added on existing algorithms including TD3BC and AWAC. Experiments on D4RL dataset show improved performances over vanilla TD3BC and AWAC.

Strengths:
The strength of policy constraints in offline RL is an important problem. It is a novel perspective to separate the policies for value estimation and inference with different constraint levels. I would say the proposition that milder constraints can improve policy inference performances is an interesting problem. The experiments are thorough with necessary ablation studies, and the results indeed show some improvement by using the proposed constraining method.

Weaknesses:
I think one major critique of the paper is: the most essential discovery that milder constraints may be required for test-time inference is mostly from experimental evaluations. The observations are not even consistent for that only 6 out of 9 shows this pattern. This is not strong evidence showing that milder constraints are necessarily always better. Some theoretical analysis or at least insights about this observation can be provided to make it more convincing.

Another critique is that although the experiments show some improvement by using MCEP on TD3BC and AWAC and over some baselines like CQL and IQL. These are not the SOTA results on these offline datasets, there exists better algorithms proposed by the time of NeurIPS submission that should be aware of:

[1] Hansen-Estruch, Philippe, et al. "Idql: Implicit q-learning as an actor-critic method with diffusion policies." arXiv preprint arXiv:2304.10573 (2023).

[2] Garg, Divyansh, et al. "Extreme Q-Learning: MaxEnt RL without Entropy." arXiv preprint arXiv:2301.02328 (2023).

[3]  Wang, Zhendong, Jonathan J. Hunt, and Mingyuan Zhou. "Diffusion policies as an expressive policy class for offline reinforcement learning." arXiv preprint arXiv:2208.06193 (2022).

The paper writing can be further improved.

Questions:
How to evaluate the strength of restriction for Eq. (8) given the fact that it has an additional $Q$ term? I think it’s not fair to just say Eq. (8) is less restrictive than Eq. (6) since after taking an exponential function it is similar as Eq. (6) but with advantage $A$ replaced by $Q$.

For Fig. 5, I think it’s better to just give the $\alpha$ values in a table.

Fig. 4, are the results averaged across different seeds?

Limitations:
The limitations of the current method are discussed, that the evaluation policies in MCEP may not be consistent with the value function and can lead to unstable value estimation if used in policy evaluation.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Peer Review

## Summary

This work addresses the challenge of policy constraints in the offline reinforcement learning paradigm, proposing that relaxing constraints during training while maintaining stricter constraints at test time can yield superior performance. The authors introduce MCEP, a flexible component compatible with existing algorithms such as TD3BC and AWAC. Empirical validation on D4RL benchmarks demonstrates performance gains relative to the baseline variants of TD3BC and AWAC.

## Strengths

The paper tackles a practically relevant and important question concerning how policy constraints affect offline RL performance. The core insight—that employing distinct constraint regimes for value function learning versus policy deployment—represents a fresh conceptual contribution. The finding that relaxed training constraints can enhance test-time policy quality is intellectually compelling and worth investigating. The experimental evaluation is comprehensive, including appropriate ablation studies that substantiate the proposed approach's benefits across multiple algorithms and environments.

## Weaknesses

A significant limitation of this work is that the primary claim regarding the benefits of milder constraints relies almost entirely on empirical observation. The supporting evidence is not even universally consistent across experimental settings, with only 6 out of 9 cases exhibiting the hypothesized pattern. This level of consistency does not constitute compelling evidence that relaxed constraints are universally beneficial. The paper would be substantially strengthened by incorporating theoretical justification or at minimum intuitive explanations that clarify why this phenomenon occurs.

Additionally, while MCEP demonstrates improvements over TD3BC, AWAC, and certain baseline methods like CQL and IQL, the results do not achieve state-of-the-art performance on the offline benchmarks examined. Several more recent algorithmic approaches that were available prior to NeurIPS submission outperform the proposed method:

[1] Hansen-Estruch, Philippe, et al. "Idql: Implicit q-learning as an actor-critic method with diffusion policies." arXiv preprint arXiv:2304.10573 (2023).

[2] Garg, Divyansh, et al. "Extreme Q-Learning: MaxEnt RL without Entropy." arXiv preprint arXiv:2301.02328 (2023).

[3] Wang, Zhendong, Jonathan J. Hunt, and Mingyuan Zhou. "Diffusion policies as an expressive policy class for offline reinforcement learning." arXiv preprint arXiv:2208.06193 (2022).

The presentation quality would benefit from revision and refinement.

## Questions and Clarifications

Regarding Equation (8), how should one assess the degree of constraint restriction when an additional Q term is introduced? The claim that Equation (8) is less restrictive than Equation (6) appears questionable, since exponentiating the expression yields a formulation analogous to Equation (6) with advantage A substituted by Q-values.

For Figure 5, presenting the α values in tabular format would enhance clarity and readability compared to the current visualization approach.

Concerning Figure 4, were the reported results obtained by averaging performance across multiple random seeds?

## Limitations

The authors appropriately acknowledge that the evaluation policies employed in MCEP may diverge from the learned value function, potentially resulting in unstable value estimation when these policies are used for value function evaluation.

## Recommendation

**6: Weak Accept** – The paper presents technically sound methodology with moderate-to-high potential impact, demonstrating no substantial concerns regarding experimental rigor, computational resources, reproducibility, or ethical dimensions. The work merits publication despite the noted limitations regarding theoretical grounding and competitive positioning relative to concurrent methods.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Summary

The paper introduces the Mildly Constrained Evaluation Policy (MCEP), a technique designed to mitigate overly stringent action selection constraints in offline reinforcement learning settings. The approach employs a more tightly constrained policy during value function updates while utilizing a comparatively less constrained policy for assessing performance. Experimental validation indicates that MCEP achieves competitive results.

## Strengths

MCEP presents a straightforward approach that can be readily integrated as a module into existing offline RL methods that rely on policy constraints.

## Weaknesses

**Computational and Conceptual Efficiency**: The design choice regarding $\pi_e$ warrants reconsideration. Since $\pi_e$ does not contribute to the policy evaluation procedure, Algorithm 1 line 7 appears redundant. A more efficient alternative would be to derive $\pi_e$ directly from the learned Q-function following the actor-critic training phase, thereby reducing unnecessary computational overhead. This observation suggests that the core contribution—essentially extracting a less constrained policy post-training—may be somewhat narrow in scope.

**Clarity and Notation Issues**: The presentation suffers from insufficient precision in several respects. The algorithmic description in Algorithm 1 employs notation ($\psi, \phi, \tilde{\pi}, \pi^e, \tilde{w}, w^e, \mathcal{L}(.,.)$) without adequate formal introduction. Additionally, there appears to be a notational inconsistency: the parameters $\psi$ and $\phi$ referenced in lines 6 and 7 of Algorithm 1 seem reversed relative to their definitions in Equation 2, where $\phi$ is associated with Q-function evaluation. These issues impede clarity and suggest incomplete technical exposition.

**Absence of Theoretical Foundation**: The paper provides no theoretical analysis or guarantees supporting the proposed method. The work would benefit substantially from formal justification of why this particular approach to policy constraint relaxation should be expected to improve performance.

## Additional Observations

**Hyperparameter Complexity**: The method introduces additional hyperparameters requiring tuning beyond those present in baseline algorithms, which increases practical implementation complexity.

## Questions

None at this time.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 3 - Reject**

The paper exhibits technical concerns including notational inconsistencies and potential implementation inefficiencies, lacks theoretical grounding, and presents a contribution that, while implementable, is limited in conceptual novelty. These factors collectively warrant rejection.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes Mildly Constrained Evaluation Policy (MCEP) for offline reinforcement learning to address the issue of excessively restrictive constraints for action selection during test time inference. MCEP uses a more constrained target policy for value estimation and another less restrictive policy for performance evaluation. Empirical results demonstrate the effectiveness of MCEP.

Strengths:
MCEP is easy to implement and can be plugged into many policy constraint offline RL methods.

Weaknesses:
Since $\pi_e$ does not participate in the policy evaluation, I think line 7 of Algorithm 1 can be removed and $\pi_e$ can be extracted from Q after actor critic learning to save computational cost. The contribution of MCEP is only to extract a less restrictive policy after RL learning, which is somewhat limited.

The overall idea of the paper is quite simple. However, the notations and descriptions are a bit confusing. For example, the notations in Algorithm 1 lack a clear definition ($\psi, \phi, \tilde \pi, \pi^e, \tilde w, w^e, \mathcal L(.,.)$). And $\psi$ and $\phi$ in line 6 and 7 of Algorithm 1 are reversed, since Q evaluation in Equation 2 is associated with $\phi$. 

No theory supports MCEP in the paper.

Questions:
I do not have additional questions.

Limitations:
More hyperparameters need to be tuned for MCEP compared with the original algorithm.

Rating:
3: Reject: For instance, a paper with technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility and incompletely addressed ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 3: Reject: For instance, a paper with technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility and incompletely addressed ethical considerations.
